**Scotland**[[e]](#cite_note-15) is a [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") that is part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It contains nearly one-third of the United Kingdom's land area, consisting of the northern part of the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and more than 790 adjacent [islands](/wiki/Islands_of_Scotland "Islands of Scotland"), principally in the archipelagos of the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides") and the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles"). In 2022, the country's population was about 5.4 million.[[11]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-16) Its capital city is [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), whilst [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") is the largest city and the most populous of the [cities of Scotland](/wiki/Cities_of_Scotland "Cities of Scotland"). Scotland shares [a land border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") with [England](/wiki/England "England") to the south-east and is surrounded by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the north and west, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the north-east and east, and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the south. The legislature, the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), elects 129 [members](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") to represent 73 [constituencies](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_Parliament_constituencies_and_electoral_regions_(2011%E2%80%932026) "List of Scottish Parliament constituencies and electoral regions (2011–2026)").[[12]](#cite_note-17) The [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") is the [executive arm](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") of the devolved government, headed by the [first minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), who chairs the [cabinet](/wiki/Scottish_Cabinet "Scottish Cabinet") and is responsible for government policy and [international engagement](/wiki/International_relations_of_Scotland "International relations of Scotland").[[13]](#cite_note-18)[[14]](#cite_note-19)

The [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") emerged as a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") in the 9th century. [Independence from England was maintained](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence") partly through an [alliance with France](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance"). In 1603, [James VI](/wiki/James_VI "James VI") succeeded to the thrones of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), forming a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") of the [three kingdoms](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"). On 1 May 1707, Scotland and England combined to create the new [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"),[[15]](#cite_note-Keay-20)[[16]](#cite_note-Mackie-21) with the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") subsumed into the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"). In 1999, a [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") was re-established, and has [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") authority over many areas of [domestic policy](/wiki/Domestic_policy "Domestic policy").[[17]](#cite_note-22) The country has its own distinct [legal system](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"), [education system](/wiki/Education_in_Scotland "Education in Scotland") and [religious history](/wiki/History_of_Christianity_in_Scotland "History of Christianity in Scotland"), which have all contributed to the continuation of [Scottish culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Scotland "Culture of Scotland") and [national identity](/wiki/Scottish_national_identity "Scottish national identity").[[18]](#cite_note-administrative_control-23) [Scottish English](/wiki/Scottish_English "Scottish English") and [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") are the most widely spoken [languages in the country](/wiki/Languages_of_Scotland "Languages of Scotland"), existing on a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") with each other.[[19]](#cite_note-24) [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") speakers can be found all over Scotland, but the language is largely spoken natively by communities within the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides");[[20]](#cite_note-25) Gaelic speakers now constitute less than 2% of the total population, although state-sponsored [revitalisation](/wiki/Revitalisation_of_Scottish_Gaelic "Revitalisation of Scottish Gaelic") attempts have led to a growing community of [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") speakers.[[21]](#cite_note-26)

The mainland of Scotland is broadly divided into three regions: the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"), a mountainous region in the north and north-west; the [Lowlands](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands"), a flatter plain across the centre of the country; and the [Southern Uplands](/wiki/Southern_Uplands "Southern Uplands"), a hilly region along the southern border. The Highlands are the most mountainous region of the British Isles and contain its highest peak, [Ben Nevis](/wiki/Ben_Nevis "Ben Nevis"), at 4,413 feet (1,345 m).[[11]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-16) The region also contains many lakes, called [lochs](/wiki/Loch "Loch"); the term is also applied to the many saltwater inlets along the country's deeply indented western coastline. The geography of the many islands is varied. Some, such as [Mull](/wiki/Isle_of_Mull "Isle of Mull") and [Skye](/wiki/Isle_of_Skye "Isle of Skye"), are noted for their mountainous terrain, while [Tiree](/wiki/Tiree "Tiree") and [Coll](/wiki/Coll "Coll") are flatter.

## Etymology

Main article: [Etymology of Scotland](/wiki/Etymology_of_Scotland "Etymology of Scotland")

*Scotland* comes from *[Scoti](/wiki/Scoti "Scoti")*, the Latin name for the [Gaels](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels").[[22]](#cite_note-27) [Philip Freeman](/wiki/Philip_Freeman "Philip Freeman") has speculated on the likelihood of a group of raiders adopting a name from an [Indo-European root](/wiki/Indo-European_root "Indo-European root"), \**skot*, citing the parallel in Greek *skotos* (σκότος), meaning 'darkness, gloom'.[[23]](#cite_note-28) The [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") word *[Scotia](/wiki/Scotia "Scotia")* ("land of the Gaels") was initially used to refer to Ireland,[[24]](#cite_note-29) and likewise in early [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") *Scotland* was used for Ireland.[[25]](#cite_note-30) By the 11th century at the latest, *Scotia* was being used to refer to (Gaelic-speaking) Scotland north of the [River Forth](/wiki/River_Forth "River Forth"), alongside *Albania* or *Albany*, both derived from the Gaelic *[Alba](/wiki/Alba "Alba")*.[[26]](#cite_note-Brewer-31) The use of the words *Scots* and *Scotland* to encompass all of what is now Scotland became common in the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Late Middle Ages").[[15]](#cite_note-Keay-20)

## History

Main article: [History of Scotland](/wiki/History_of_Scotland "History of Scotland")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Scottish history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Scottish_history "Timeline of Scottish history").

### Prehistory

The earliest known evidence of human presence in Scotland is [Hamburgian culture](/wiki/Hamburgian_culture "Hamburgian culture") stone tools produced by late [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") [hunter gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") who arrived in Scotland during the [Bølling–Allerød Interstadial](/wiki/B%C3%B8lling%E2%80%93Aller%C3%B8d_Interstadial "Bølling–Allerød Interstadial") warm period at the end of the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_ice_age "Last ice age"), around 14,500 to 14,000 years ago, shortly following the retreat of the ice sheet that had previously covered Scotland.[[27]](#cite_note-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33) [Neolithic farmers](/wiki/Early_European_Farmers "Early European Farmers") arrived in Scotland around 6000 years ago.[[29]](#cite_note-34) The well-preserved village of [Skara Brae](/wiki/Skara_Brae "Skara Brae") on the mainland of [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") dates from this period. Neolithic habitation, burial, and ritual sites are particularly common and well preserved in the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles") and [Western Isles](/wiki/Western_Isles "Western Isles"), where a lack of trees led to most structures being built of local stone.[[30]](#cite_note-35) Evidence of sophisticated pre-Christian belief systems is demonstrated by sites such as the [Callanish Stones](/wiki/Callanish_Stones "Callanish Stones") on [Lewis](/wiki/Isle_of_Lewis "Isle of Lewis"), and the [Maes Howe](/wiki/Maes_Howe "Maes Howe") on [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney"), which were built in the third millennium BC.[[31]](#cite_note-short-36): 38

### Early history

Main articles: [Scotland during the Roman Empire](/wiki/Scotland_during_the_Roman_Empire "Scotland during the Roman Empire"), [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), and [Pictland](/wiki/Pictland "Pictland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Skara_Brae_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3912232.jpg/250px-Skara_Brae_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3912232.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skara_Brae_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3912232.jpg)

[Skara Brae](/wiki/Skara_Brae "Skara Brae"), Europe's most complete [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") village, occupied from roughly 3180 BC – 2500 BC

The first written reference to Scotland was in 320 BC by Greek sailor [Pytheas](/wiki/Pytheas "Pytheas"), who called the northern tip of Britain "Orcas", the source of the name of the Orkney islands.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 10

Most of modern Scotland was not [incorporated](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain") into the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), and Roman control over parts of the area fluctuated over a rather short period. The first Roman incursion into Scotland was in 79 AD, when [Agricola](/wiki/Gnaeus_Julius_Agricola "Gnaeus Julius Agricola") invaded Scotland; he defeated a Caledonian army at the [Battle of Mons Graupius](/wiki/Battle_of_Mons_Graupius "Battle of Mons Graupius") in 83 AD.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 12 After the Roman victory, Roman forts were briefly set along the [Gask Ridge](/wiki/Gask_Ridge "Gask Ridge") close to the [Highland line](/wiki/Highland_Boundary_Fault "Highland Boundary Fault"), but by three years after the battle, the Roman armies had withdrawn to the [Southern Uplands](/wiki/Southern_Uplands "Southern Uplands").[[33]](#cite_note-38) Remains of Roman forts established in the 1st century have been found as far north as the [Moray Firth](/wiki/Moray_Firth "Moray Firth").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) By the reign of the [Roman emperor](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor") [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") (r. 98–117), Roman control had lapsed to Britain south of a line between the [River Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and the [Solway Firth](/wiki/Solway_Firth "Solway Firth").[[35]](#cite_note-:8-40) Along this line, Trajan's successor [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") (r. 117–138) erected [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") in northern England[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 12 and the *[Limes Britannicus](/wiki/Limes_Britannicus "Limes Britannicus")* became the northern border of the Roman Empire.[[36]](#cite_note-41)[[37]](#cite_note-42) The Roman influence on the southern part of the country was considerable, and they introduced [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") to Scotland.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 13–14[[31]](#cite_note-short-36): 38

The [Antonine Wall](/wiki/Antonine_Wall "Antonine Wall") was built from 142 at the order of Hadrian's successor [Antoninus Pius](/wiki/Antoninus_Pius "Antoninus Pius") (r. 138–161), defending the Roman part of Scotland from the unadministered part of the island, north of a line between the [Firth of Clyde](/wiki/Firth_of_Clyde "Firth of Clyde") and the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth").[[38]](#cite_note-43) The [Roman invasion of Caledonia 208–210](/wiki/Roman_invasion_of_Caledonia_208%E2%80%93210 "Roman invasion of Caledonia 208–210") was undertaken by emperors of the imperial [Severan dynasty](/wiki/Severan_dynasty "Severan dynasty") in response to the breaking of a treaty by the Caledonians in 197,[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) but permanent conquest of the whole of Great Britain was forestalled by Roman forces becoming bogged down in punishing [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") and the death of the senior emperor [Septimius Severus](/wiki/Septimius_Severus "Septimius Severus") (r. 193–211) at [Eboracum](/wiki/Eboracum "Eboracum") ([York](/wiki/York "York")) after he was taken ill while on campaign. Although forts erected by the [Roman army](/wiki/Roman_army "Roman army") in the Severan campaign were placed near those established by Agricola and were clustered at the mouths of the [glens](/wiki/Glens "Glens") in the Highlands, the Caledonians were again in revolt in 210–211 and these were overrun.[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Callanish_at_sunset_-_geograph.org.uk_-_820680.jpg/250px-Callanish_at_sunset_-_geograph.org.uk_-_820680.jpg)](/wiki/File:Callanish_at_sunset_-_geograph.org.uk_-_820680.jpg)

[Callanish Stones](/wiki/Callanish_Stones "Callanish Stones"), erected in the late [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") era

To the Roman historians [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus") and [Cassius Dio](/wiki/Cassius_Dio "Cassius Dio"), the [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") and the area north of the [River Forth](/wiki/River_Forth "River Forth") was called [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia "Caledonia").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) According to Cassius Dio, the inhabitants of Caledonia were the [Caledonians](/wiki/Caledonians "Caledonians") and the [Maeatae](/wiki/Maeatae "Maeatae").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) Other ancient authors used the adjective "Caledonian" to mean anywhere in northern or inland Britain, often mentioning the region's people and animals, its cold climate, its [pearls](/wiki/Pearl "Pearl"), and a noteworthy region of wooded hills ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *saltus*) which the 2nd century AD Roman philosopher [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"), in his [*Geography*](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)"), described as being south-west of the [Beauly Firth](/wiki/Beauly_Firth "Beauly Firth").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) The name Caledonia is echoed in the place names of [Dunkeld](/wiki/Dunkeld "Dunkeld"), [Rohallion](/wiki/Rohallion "Rohallion"), and [Schiehallion](/wiki/Schiehallion "Schiehallion").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39)

The [Great Conspiracy](/wiki/Great_Conspiracy "Great Conspiracy") constituted a seemingly coordinated invasion against Roman rule in Britain in the later 4th century, which included the participation of the Gaelic [Scoti](/wiki/Scoti "Scoti") and the Caledonians, who were then known as [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts") by the Romans. This was defeated by the [*comes* Theodosius](/wiki/Comes_Theodosius "Comes Theodosius"); but Roman military government was withdrawn from the island altogether by the early 5th century, resulting in the [Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") and the immigration of the [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") to southeastern Scotland and the rest of eastern Great Britain.[[35]](#cite_note-:8-40)

### Kingdom of Scotland

Main articles: [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), [Scotland in the Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Early Middle Ages"), [Scotland in the High Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_High_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the High Middle Ages"), and [Scotland in the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Late Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Early_Medieval_Scotland_areas.png/250px-Early_Medieval_Scotland_areas.png)](/wiki/File:Early_Medieval_Scotland_areas.png)

Political divisions in early medieval Scotland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Kingdom_of_Mann_and_the_Isles-en.svg/250px-Kingdom_of_Mann_and_the_Isles-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Mann_and_the_Isles-en.svg)

Norse kingdoms at the end of the eleventh century

Beginning in the sixth century, the area that is now Scotland was divided into four areas: [Pictland](/wiki/Pictland "Pictland"), a patchwork of small lordships in central Scotland;[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 25–26 the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"), which had conquered southeastern Scotland;[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 18–20 Northern Brittonic territory likely centred on [Alt Clut](/wiki/Alt_Clut "Alt Clut") (Dumbarton Rock) and the Clyde valley;[[39]](#cite_note-44) and [Dál Riata](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_Riata "Dál Riata"), which included territory in western Scotland and northern Ireland, and spread Gaelic language and culture into Scotland.[[40]](#cite_note-45) These societies were based on the family unit and had sharp divisions in wealth, although the vast majority were poor and worked full-time in [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture"). The Picts kept slaves (mostly captured in war) through the ninth century.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 26–27

Gaelic influence over Pictland and Northumbria was facilitated by the large number of [Gaelic-speaking clerics](/wiki/Celtic_Church "Celtic Church") working as missionaries.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 23–24 Operating in the sixth century on the island of [Iona](/wiki/Iona "Iona"), [Saint Columba](/wiki/Saint_Columba "Saint Columba") was one of the earliest and best-known missionaries.[[31]](#cite_note-short-36): 39 The [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") began to raid Scotland in the eighth century. Although the raiders sought slaves and luxury items, their main motivation was to acquire land. The oldest [Norse](/wiki/Norse_Scotland "Norse Scotland") settlements were in northwest Scotland, but they eventually conquered many areas along the coast. [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") entirely displaced [Pictish](/wiki/Pictish_language "Pictish language") in the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles").[[41]](#cite_note-46)

In the ninth century, the Norse threat allowed a Gael named [Kenneth I](/wiki/Kenneth_I "Kenneth I") (Cináed mac Ailpín) to seize power over Pictland, establishing a royal dynasty to which the modern monarchs trace their lineage, and marking the beginning of the end of Pictish culture.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 31–32[[42]](#cite_note-47) The kingdom of Cináed and his descendants, called Alba, was Gaelic in character but existed on the same area as Pictland. By the end of the tenth century, the Pictish language went extinct as its speakers shifted to Gaelic.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 32–33 From a base in eastern Scotland north of the [River Forth](/wiki/River_Forth "River Forth") and south of the [River Spey](/wiki/River_Spey "River Spey"), the kingdom expanded first southwards, into the former Northumbrian lands, and northwards into [Moray](/wiki/Province_of_Moray "Province of Moray").[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 34–35 Around the turn of the millennium, there was a centralization in agricultural lands and the first towns began to be established.[[32]](#cite_note-Forsyth-37): 36–37

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Great_Window%2C_Parliament_Hall%2C_Edinburgh.JPG/250px-Great_Window%2C_Parliament_Hall%2C_Edinburgh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Great_Window,_Parliament_Hall,_Edinburgh.JPG)

[James V of Scotland](/wiki/James_V_of_Scotland "James V of Scotland") at the [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session") in 1532, at [Parliament House, Edinburgh](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh"), the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") until 1707

In the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, much of Scotland was under the control of a single ruler. Initially, Gaelic culture predominated, but immigrants from France, England and Flanders steadily created a more diverse society, with the Gaelic language starting to be replaced by Scots; and a modern nation-state emerged from this. At the end of this period, war against England started the growth of a [Scottish national consciousness](/wiki/Scottish_national_consciousness "Scottish national consciousness").[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48)[[44]](#cite_note-Barrell_2000-49): ch 1 [David I](/wiki/David_I_of_Scotland "David I of Scotland") (1124–1153) and his successors centralised royal power[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48): 41–42 and united mainland Scotland, capturing regions such as Moray, [Galloway](/wiki/Galloway "Galloway"), and [Caithness](/wiki/Caithness "Caithness"), although he could not extend his power over the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides"), which had been ruled by various [Scottish clans](/wiki/Scottish_clans "Scottish clans") following the death of [Somerled](/wiki/Somerled "Somerled") in 1164.[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48): 48–49 In 1266, Scotland fought the short but consequential [Scottish-Norwegian War](/wiki/Scottish-Norwegian_War "Scottish-Norwegian War") which saw the reclamation of the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides") after the strong defeat of King [Haakon IV](/wiki/Haakon_IV "Haakon IV") and his forces at the [Battle of Largs](/wiki/Battle_of_Largs "Battle of Largs").[[45]](#cite_note-50) Up until that point, the Hebrides had been under Norwegian Viking control for roughly 400 years and had developed a distinctive [Norse–Gaelic](/wiki/Norse%E2%80%93Gaelic "Norse–Gaelic") culture that saw many [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") loanwords enter the [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") spoken by islanders, and through successive generations the Norse would become almost completely assimilated into [Gaelic culture](/wiki/Gaelic_culture "Gaelic culture") and the [Scottish clan](/wiki/Scottish_clan "Scottish clan") system. After the conflict, Scotland had to affirm Norwegian sovereignty of the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles"), but they were later integrated into Scotland in the 15th century. Scandinavian culture in the form of the [Norn language](/wiki/Norn_language "Norn language") survived for a lot longer than in the Hebrides, and would strongly influence the local [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") dialect on [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") and [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney").[[46]](#cite_note-51) Later, a system of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") was consolidated, with both Anglo-Norman incomers and native Gaelic chieftains being granted land in exchange for serving the king.[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48): 53–54 The relationship with [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") was complex during this period: Scottish kings tried several times, sometimes with success, to exploit English political turmoil, followed by the longest period of peace between Scotland and England in the mediaeval period: from 1217–1296.[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48): 45-46

### Wars of Scottish Independence

Main articles: [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence"), [First War of Scottish Independence](/wiki/First_War_of_Scottish_Independence "First War of Scottish Independence"), and [Second War of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Second_War_of_Scottish_Independence "Second War of Scottish Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Wallace_Monument_%2C_Stirling%2C_Scotland%2C_in_Autumn.jpg/250px-Wallace_Monument_%2C_Stirling%2C_Scotland%2C_in_Autumn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wallace_Monument_,_Stirling,_Scotland,_in_Autumn.jpg)

The [Wallace Monument](/wiki/Wallace_Monument "Wallace Monument") in [Stirling](/wiki/Stirling "Stirling"), commemorates Sir [William Wallace](/wiki/William_Wallace "William Wallace"), a Scottish independence leader[[47]](#cite_note-52)

The death of [Alexander III](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") in March 1286 broke the succession line of Scotland's kings. [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") arbitrated between various claimants for the Scottish crown. In return for surrendering Scotland's nominal independence, [John Balliol](/wiki/John_Balliol "John Balliol") was pronounced king in 1292.[[43]](#cite_note-Stringer-48): 47[[48]](#cite_note-Scotland_Conquered-53) In 1294, Balliol and other Scottish lords refused Edward's demands to serve in his army against the French. Scotland and France sealed a treaty on 23 October 1295, known as the [Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance"). War ensued, and John was deposed by Edward who took personal control of Scotland. [Andrew Moray](/wiki/Andrew_Moray "Andrew Moray") and [William Wallace](/wiki/William_Wallace "William Wallace") initially emerged as the principal leaders of the resistance to English rule in the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence"),[[49]](#cite_note-54) until [Robert the Bruce](/wiki/Robert_the_Bruce "Robert the Bruce") was crowned king of Scotland in 1306.[[50]](#cite_note-55) Victory at the [Battle of Bannockburn](/wiki/Battle_of_Bannockburn "Battle of Bannockburn") in 1314 proved the Scots had regained control of their kingdom. In 1320 the world's first documented declaration of independence, the [Declaration of Arbroath](/wiki/Declaration_of_Arbroath "Declaration of Arbroath"), won the support of [Pope John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII"), leading to the legal recognition of Scottish sovereignty by the English Crown.[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-56): 70, 72

A civil war between the [Bruce dynasty](/wiki/Bruce_dynasty "Bruce dynasty") and their long-term rivals of the [House of Comyn](/wiki/House_of_Comyn "House of Comyn") and [House of Balliol](/wiki/House_of_Balliol "House of Balliol") lasted until the middle of the 14th century. Although the Bruce faction was successful, [David II](/wiki/David_II_of_Scotland "David II of Scotland")'s lack of an heir allowed his half-nephew [Robert II](/wiki/Robert_II_of_Scotland "Robert II of Scotland"), the [Lord High Steward of Scotland](/wiki/Lord_High_Steward_of_Scotland "Lord High Steward of Scotland"), to come to the throne and establish the [House of Stewart](/wiki/House_of_Stewart "House of Stewart").[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-56): 77 The Stewarts ruled Scotland for the remainder of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). The country they ruled experienced greater prosperity from the end of the 14th century through the Scottish Renaissance to the [Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation"),[[52]](#cite_note-Mason-57): 93 despite the effects of the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") in 1349[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-56): 76 and increasing division between [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") and [Lowlands](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands").[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-56): 78 Multiple truces reduced warfare on the southern border.[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-56): 76, 83

### Union of the Crowns

Main articles: [Scotland in the early modern period](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_early_modern_period "Scotland in the early modern period") and [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Adam_de_Colone_%28c.1572-1651%29_-_James_VI_and_I_%281566%E2%80%931625%29%2C_King_of_Scotland_%281567%E2%80%931625%29%2C_King_of_England_and_Ireland_%281603%E2%80%931625%29_-_PG_2172_-_National_Galleries_of_Scotland.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adam_de_Colone_(c.1572-1651)_-_James_VI_and_I_(1566%E2%80%931625),_King_of_Scotland_(1567%E2%80%931625),_King_of_England_and_Ireland_(1603%E2%80%931625)_-_PG_2172_-_National_Galleries_of_Scotland.jpg)

[James VI](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), [King of Scotland](/wiki/King_of_Scotland "King of Scotland"), succeeded to the English and Irish thrones in 1603.

The [Treaty of Perpetual Peace](/wiki/Treaty_of_Perpetual_Peace "Treaty of Perpetual Peace") was signed in 1502 by [James IV of Scotland](/wiki/James_IV_of_Scotland "James IV of Scotland") and [Henry VII of England](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"). James married Henry's daughter, [Margaret Tudor](/wiki/Margaret_Tudor "Margaret Tudor").[[53]](#cite_note-58) James invaded England in support of France under the terms of the [Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance") and became the last monarch in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") to die in battle, at [Flodden](/wiki/Battle_of_Flodden "Battle of Flodden") in 1513.[[54]](#cite_note-59) The war with England during the minority years of [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots") between 1543 and 1551 is known as the [Rough Wooing](/wiki/Rough_Wooing "Rough Wooing").[[55]](#cite_note-60) In 1560, the [Treaty of Edinburgh](/wiki/Treaty_of_Edinburgh "Treaty of Edinburgh") brought an end to the [Siege of Leith](/wiki/Siege_of_Leith "Siege of Leith") and recognized the Protestant [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") as Queen of England.[[52]](#cite_note-Mason-57): 112 The [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation_Parliament "Scottish Reformation Parliament") met and immediately adopted the [Scots Confession](/wiki/Scots_Confession "Scots Confession"), which signalled the [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation")'s sharp break from papal authority and Roman Catholic teaching.[[31]](#cite_note-short-36): 44 The Catholic Mary, Queen of Scots, was forced to [abdicate in 1567](/wiki/Act_Anent_the_demission_of_the_Crown_in_favour_of_our_Sovereign_Lord,_and_his_Majesty%27s_Coronation_1567 "Act Anent the demission of the Crown in favour of our Sovereign Lord, and his Majesty's Coronation 1567").[[56]](#cite_note-61)

In 1603, [James VI, King of Scots](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") inherited the thrones of the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") in the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), and moved to London.[[57]](#cite_note-62) This was a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") as despite having the same monarch the kingdoms retained their separate parliaments, laws and other institutions. The first [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") was designed at James's behest, to be flown in addition to the [St Andrew's Cross](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") on Scots vessels at sea. James VI and I intended to create a single kingdom of Great Britain, but was thwarted in his attempt to do so by the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), which supported the wrecking proposal that a full legal union be sought instead, a proposal to which the Scots Parliament would not assent, causing the king to withdraw the plan.[[58]](#cite_note-63)

Except for a short period under [the Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate"), Scotland remained a separate state in the 17th century, but there was considerable conflict between the crown and the [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanters "Covenanters") over the form of [church government](/wiki/Presbyterian_church_governance "Presbyterian church governance").[[59]](#cite_note-Wormald-64): 124 The military was strengthened, allowing the imposition of royal authority on the western Highland clans. The 1609 [Statutes of Iona](/wiki/Statutes_of_Iona "Statutes of Iona") compelled the cultural integration of Hebridean clan leaders.[[60]](#cite_note-65): 37–40 In 1641 and again in 1643, the Parliament of Scotland unsuccessfully sought a union with England which was "federative" and not "incorporating", in which Scotland would retain a separate parliament.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) The issue of union split the parliament in 1648.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66)

After the execution of the Scottish king at [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall") in 1649, amid the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms") and [its events in Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), the victorious [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector"), imposed the British Isles' first written constitution – the [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government "Instrument of Government") – on Scotland in 1652 as part of the republican [Commonwealth of England, Scotland, and Ireland](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England,_Scotland,_and_Ireland "Commonwealth of England, Scotland, and Ireland").[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) The Protectorate Parliament was the first Westminster parliament to include representatives nominally from Scotland. The monarchy of the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") was resumed with the [Restoration in Scotland](/wiki/Restoration_in_Scotland "Restoration in Scotland") in 1660. The Parliament of Scotland sought a commercial union with England in 1664; the proposal was rejected in 1668.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) In 1670 the Parliament of England rejected a proposed political union with Scotland.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) English proposals along the same lines were abandoned in 1674 and in 1685.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) The Scots Parliament rejected proposals for a political union with England in 1689.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) [Jacobitism](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism"), the political support for the exiled Catholic Stuart dynasty, remained a threat to the security of the British state under the Protestant [House of Orange](/wiki/House_of_Orange "House of Orange") and the succeeding [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") until the defeat of the [Jacobite rising of 1745](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745").[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) In 1698, the [Company of Scotland](/wiki/Company_of_Scotland "Company of Scotland") attempted a project to secure a trading colony on the [Isthmus of Panama](/wiki/Isthmus_of_Panama "Isthmus of Panama"). Almost every Scottish landowner who had money to spare is said to have invested in the [Darien scheme](/wiki/Darien_scheme "Darien scheme").[[62]](#cite_note-:6-67)[[63]](#cite_note-:7-68)

### Treaty of Union

Main article: [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union")

Further information: [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Treaty_of_Union.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Union.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Union.jpg)

Scottish Exemplification (official copy) of the Treaty of Union of 1707

After another proposal from the English House of Lords was rejected in 1695, and a further Lords motion was voted down in the House of Commons in 1700, the Parliament of Scotland again rejected union in 1702.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66) The failure of the Darien Scheme bankrupted the landowners who had invested, though not the burghs. Nevertheless, the nobles' bankruptcy, along with the threat of an English invasion, played a leading role in convincing the Scots elite to back a union with England.[[62]](#cite_note-:6-67)[[63]](#cite_note-:7-68) On 22 July 1706, the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") was agreed between representatives of the [Scots Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") and the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"). The following year, twin [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") were passed by both parliaments to create the united [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") with effect from 1 May 1707[[64]](#cite_note-69) with popular opposition and anti-union riots in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), and elsewhere.[[65]](#cite_note-1707_protests-70)[[66]](#cite_note-71) The union also created the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), which succeeded both the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") and the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), which rejected proposals from the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") that the third kingdom be incorporated in the union.[[61]](#cite_note-:5-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/JamesFirstEarlOfSeafield.jpg/250px-JamesFirstEarlOfSeafield.jpg)](/wiki/File:JamesFirstEarlOfSeafield.jpg)

[James Ogilvy](/wiki/James_Ogilvy,_4th_Earl_of_Findlater "James Ogilvy, 4th Earl of Findlater"), former [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor_of_Scotland "Lord Chancellor of Scotland") (1702–1708), initially supported union but by 1713 advocated for its reversal

[Andrew Fletcher](/wiki/Andrew_Fletcher_(patriot) "Andrew Fletcher (patriot)"), a prominent Scottish patriot, argued that the ratification of the treaty would see Scotland "more like a conquered province",[[67]](#cite_note-auto3-72) and by 1713, the former [Lord Chancellor of Scotland](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor_of_Scotland "Lord Chancellor of Scotland"), [James Ogilvy, 4th Earl of Findlater](/wiki/James_Ogilvy,_4th_Earl_of_Findlater "James Ogilvy, 4th Earl of Findlater"), who was a prominent supporter for the Treaty of Union between Scotland and England had changed his position on the treaty, and unsuccessfully advocated for the treaty to be reversed.[[68]](#cite_note-73) The deposed [Jacobite Stuart](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") claimants had remained popular in the Highlands and north-east, particularly among non-[Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), including Roman Catholics and [Episcopalian Protestants](/wiki/Episcopalian_Protestants "Episcopalian Protestants"). Two major Jacobite risings launched in [1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") and [1745](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745") failed to remove the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") from the British throne. The threat of the Jacobite movement to the United Kingdom and its monarchs effectively ended at the [Battle of Culloden](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden"), Great Britain's last [pitched battle](/wiki/Pitched_battle "Pitched battle").

The passing of the Treaty of Union did not bring about immediate economic prosperity to Scotland as was widely speculated by the [pamphleteer](/wiki/Pamphleteer "Pamphleteer") as a result of the little consideration given to prospects of the Scottish economy.[[69]](#cite_note-74) Campaigners for the union between Scotland and England believed that there would be economic advantages to Scotland as a result of the failed [Darien scheme](/wiki/Darien_scheme "Darien scheme") which left the Kingdom of Scotland bankrupt.[[67]](#cite_note-auto3-72) Eventually however, with trade tariffs with England abolished, trade blossomed, especially with [Colonial America](/wiki/Colonial_America "Colonial America"). The clippers belonging to the Glasgow [Tobacco Lords](/wiki/Tobacco_Lords "Tobacco Lords") were the fastest ships on the route to [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"). Until the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence") in 1776, Glasgow was the world's premier tobacco port, dominating world trade.[[70]](#cite_note-75) The disparity between the wealth of the merchant classes of the Scottish Lowlands and the ancient clans of the Scottish Highlands grew, amplifying centuries of division.

In the Highlands, clan chiefs gradually started to think of themselves more as commercial landlords than leaders of their people. These social and economic changes included the first phase of the [Highland Clearances](/wiki/Highland_Clearances "Highland Clearances") and, ultimately, the demise of clanship.[[71]](#cite_note-Devine_1994-76): 32–53, *passim*

### Industrial age and the Scottish Enlightenment

Main article: [Scotland in the modern era](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_modern_era "Scotland in the modern era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Sir_Henry_Raeburn_-_Portrait_of_Sir_Walter_Scott.jpg/250px-Sir_Henry_Raeburn_-_Portrait_of_Sir_Walter_Scott.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Henry_Raeburn_-_Portrait_of_Sir_Walter_Scott.jpg)

[Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"), whose [Waverley Novels](/wiki/Waverley_Novels "Waverley Novels") helped define Scottish identity in the 19th century

The [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment") and the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") turned Scotland into an intellectual, commercial and industrial powerhouse[[72]](#cite_note-77) — so much so [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire") said "We look to Scotland for all our ideas of civilisation."[[73]](#cite_note-78) With the demise of Jacobitism and the advent of the Union, thousands of Scots, mainly Lowlanders, took up numerous positions of power in politics, civil service, the army and navy, trade, economics, colonial enterprises and other areas across the nascent [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). Historian Neil Davidson notes "after 1746 there was an entirely new level of participation by Scots in political life, particularly outside Scotland." Davidson also states "far from being 'peripheral' to the British economy, Scotland – or more precisely, the Lowlands – lay at its core."[[74]](#cite_note-79)

The [Scottish Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Scottish_Reform_Act_1832 "Scottish Reform Act 1832") increased the number of Scottish MPs and widened the franchise to include more of the middle classes.[[75]](#cite_note-Devine&Finlay1996pp64-5-80) From the mid-century, there were increasing calls for Home Rule for Scotland and the post of [Secretary of State for Scotland](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Scotland "Secretary of State for Scotland") was revived.[[76]](#cite_note-81) Towards the end of the century prime ministers of Scottish descent included [William Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone"),[[77]](#cite_note-Quinault2007-82) and [the Earl of Rosebery](/wiki/Archibald_Primrose,_5th_Earl_of_Rosebery "Archibald Primrose, 5th Earl of Rosebery").[[78]](#cite_note-83) In the late 19th century the growing importance of the working classes was marked by [Keir Hardie](/wiki/Keir_Hardie "Keir Hardie")'s success in the [Mid Lanarkshire by-election, 1888](/wiki/Mid_Lanarkshire_by-election,_1888 "Mid Lanarkshire by-election, 1888"), leading to the foundation of the [Scottish Labour Party](/wiki/Scottish_Labour_Party_(1888) "Scottish Labour Party (1888)"), which was absorbed into the [Independent Labour Party](/wiki/Independent_Labour_Party "Independent Labour Party") in 1895, with Hardie as its first leader.[[79]](#cite_note-84) Glasgow became one of the largest cities in the world and known as "the [Second City of the Empire](/wiki/Second_City_of_the_Empire "Second City of the Empire")" after London.[[80]](#cite_note-85) After 1860, the Clydeside shipyards specialised in steamships made of iron (after 1870, made of steel), which rapidly replaced the wooden sailing vessels of both the merchant fleets and the battle fleets of the world. It became the world's pre-eminent shipbuilding centre.[[81]](#cite_note-Shields1949-86) The industrial developments, while they brought work and wealth, were so rapid that housing, town planning, and provision for public health did not keep pace with them, and for a time living conditions in some of the towns and cities were notoriously bad, with overcrowding, high infant mortality, and growing rates of tuberculosis.[[82]](#cite_note-87)

While the Scottish Enlightenment is traditionally considered to have concluded toward the end of the 18th century,[[83]](#cite_note-Magnusson-88) disproportionately large Scottish contributions to British science and letters continued for another 50 years or more, thanks to such figures as the physicists [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell") and [Lord Kelvin](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin"), and the engineers and inventors [James Watt](/wiki/James_Watt "James Watt") and [William Murdoch](/wiki/William_Murdoch "William Murdoch"), whose work was critical to the technological developments of the Industrial Revolution throughout Britain.[[84]](#cite_note-89) In literature, the most successful figure of the mid-19th century was [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"). His first prose work, *[Waverley](/wiki/Waverley_(novel) "Waverley (novel)")* in 1814, is often called the first historical novel.[[85]](#cite_note-90) It launched a highly successful career that probably more than any other helped define and popularise Scottish cultural identity.[[86]](#cite_note-91) In the late 19th century, a number of Scottish-born authors achieved international reputations, such as [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"), [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle"), [J. M. Barrie](/wiki/J._M._Barrie "J. M. Barrie") and [George MacDonald](/wiki/George_MacDonald "George MacDonald").[[87]](#cite_note-Cultural_Profile-92) Scotland also played a major part in the development of art and architecture. The [Glasgow School](/wiki/Glasgow_School "Glasgow School"), which developed in the late 19th century, and flourished in the early 20th century, produced a distinctive blend of influences including the [Celtic Revival](/wiki/Celtic_Revival "Celtic Revival") the [Arts and Crafts movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_movement "Arts and Crafts movement"), and [Japonism](/wiki/Japonism "Japonism"), which found favour throughout the [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") world of continental Europe and helped define the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") style. Proponents included architect and artist [Charles Rennie Mackintosh](/wiki/Charles_Rennie_Mackintosh "Charles Rennie Mackintosh").[[88]](#cite_note-93)

### World wars and Scotland Act 1998

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Bagpiper_Battle_of_Bazentin_Ridge_14-07-1916_IWM_Q_4012.jpg/250px-Bagpiper_Battle_of_Bazentin_Ridge_14-07-1916_IWM_Q_4012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bagpiper_Battle_of_Bazentin_Ridge_14-07-1916_IWM_Q_4012.jpg)

A piper of the [Seaforth Highlanders](/wiki/Seaforth_Highlanders "Seaforth Highlanders") leads the [26th Brigade](/wiki/26th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "26th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") back from the trenches during the [Battle of Bazentin Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Bazentin_Ridge "Battle of Bazentin Ridge"), July 1916

Scotland played a major role in the [British effort](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_the_First_World_War "History of the United Kingdom during the First World War") in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). It especially provided manpower, ships, machinery, fish and money.[[89]](#cite_note-94) With a population of 4.8 million in 1911, Scotland sent over half a million men to the war, of whom over a quarter died in combat or from disease, and 150,000 were seriously wounded.[[90]](#cite_note-95) [Field Marshal](/wiki/Field_Marshal_(United_Kingdom) "Field Marshal (United Kingdom)") [Sir Douglas Haig](/wiki/Douglas_Haig "Douglas Haig") was Britain's commander on the Western Front. The war saw the emergence of a radical movement called "[Red Clydeside](/wiki/Red_Clydeside "Red Clydeside")" led by militant trades unionists. Formerly a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") stronghold, the industrial districts switched to [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") by 1922, with a base among the [Irish Catholic](/wiki/Irish_Catholic "Irish Catholic") working-class districts. Women were especially active in building neighbourhood solidarity on housing issues. The "Reds" operated within the Labour Party with little influence in Parliament and the mood changed to passive despair by the late 1920s.[[91]](#cite_note-96)

During the Second World War, Scotland was targeted by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") largely due to its factories, shipyards, and coal mines.[[92]](#cite_note-blitz-97) Cities such as [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") and [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") were targeted by German bombers, as were smaller towns mostly located in the central belt of the country.[[92]](#cite_note-blitz-97) Perhaps the most significant air raid in Scotland was the [Clydebank Blitz](/wiki/Clydebank_Blitz "Clydebank Blitz") of March 1941, which intended to destroy naval shipbuilding in the area.[[93]](#cite_note-Clydebank_blitz-98) 528 people were killed and 4,000 homes destroyed.[[93]](#cite_note-Clydebank_blitz-98) Perhaps Scotland's most unusual wartime episode occurred in 1941 when [Rudolf Hess](/wiki/Rudolf_Hess "Rudolf Hess") flew to Renfrewshire, possibly intending to broker a peace deal through the [Duke of Hamilton](/wiki/Douglas_Douglas-Hamilton,_14th_Duke_of_Hamilton "Douglas Douglas-Hamilton, 14th Duke of Hamilton").[[94]](#cite_note-99) Before his departure from Germany, Hess had given his adjutant, [Karlheinz Pintsch](/wiki/Karlheinz_Pintsch "Karlheinz Pintsch"), a letter addressed to [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") that detailed his intentions to open peace negotiations with the British. Pintsch delivered the letter to Hitler at the Berghof around noon on 11 May.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008168-100) [Albert Speer](/wiki/Albert_Speer "Albert Speer") later said Hitler described Hess's departure as one of the worst personal blows of his life, as he considered it a personal betrayal.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESereny1996240-101) Hitler worried that his allies, Italy and Japan, would perceive Hess's act as an attempt by Hitler to secretly open peace negotiations with the British.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament%2C_1999.jpg/250px-Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament%2C_1999.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament,_1999.jpg)

Reconvening of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") in July 1999 with [Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar"), [first minister](/wiki/First_minister_of_Scotland "First minister of Scotland") (left) and [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") (centre)

After 1945, Scotland's economic situation worsened due to overseas competition, inefficient industry, and industrial disputes.[[97]](#cite_note-102) Only in recent decades has the country enjoyed something of a cultural and economic renaissance. Economic factors contributing to this recovery included a resurgent financial services industry, [electronics manufacturing](/wiki/Electronics_manufacturing "Electronics manufacturing"), (see [Silicon Glen](/wiki/Silicon_Glen "Silicon Glen")),[[98]](#cite_note-103) and the [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") and gas industry.[[99]](#cite_note-104) The introduction in 1989 by Margaret Thatcher's government of the [Community Charge](/wiki/Poll_tax_(Great_Britain) "Poll tax (Great Britain)") (widely known as the Poll Tax) one year before the rest of Great Britain,[[100]](#cite_note-105) contributed to a growing movement for Scottish control over domestic affairs.[[101]](#cite_note-106) On 21 December 1988, [Pan Am Flight 103](/wiki/Pan_Am_Flight_103 "Pan Am Flight 103") exploded mid–air over the town of [Lockerbie](/wiki/Lockerbie "Lockerbie"), killing all on board as well as eleven Lockerbie residents. It remains the deadliest [terrorist attack in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Terrorism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Terrorism in the United Kingdom").[[102]](#cite_note-107)

Following a [referendum on devolution proposals in 1997](/wiki/Scottish_devolution_referendum,_1997 "Scottish devolution referendum, 1997"), the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998")[[103]](#cite_note-108) was passed by the British Parliament, which established a devolved [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") and [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") with responsibility for most laws specific to Scotland.[[104]](#cite_note-109) The Scottish Parliament was reconvened in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") on 4 July 1999.[[105]](#cite_note-110) The first to hold the office of [first minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_minister_of_Scotland "First minister of Scotland") was [Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar"), who served until his sudden death in 2000.[[106]](#cite_note-111)

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Signing_of_the_Edinburgh_Agreement%2C_2012.jpg/250px-Signing_of_the_Edinburgh_Agreement%2C_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Signing_of_the_Edinburgh_Agreement,_2012.jpg)

The signing of the [Edinburgh Agreement](/wiki/Edinburgh_Agreement_(2012) "Edinburgh Agreement (2012)") permitted a [referendum on independence](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") to be held in 2014

The [Scottish Parliament Building](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_Building "Scottish Parliament Building") at Holyrood opened in October 2004 after lengthy construction delays and running over budget.[[107]](#cite_note-112) The Scottish Parliament's form of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") (the [additional member system](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system")) resulted in no one party having an overall majority for the first three Scottish parliament elections.

The pro-[independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence") [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") led by [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond") achieved an overall majority in the [2011 election](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_election "Scottish Parliament election"), winning 69 of the 129 seats available.[[108]](#cite_note-113) The success of the SNP in achieving a majority in the Scottish Parliament paved the way for the [September 2014 referendum on Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence_referendum,_2014 "Scottish independence referendum, 2014"). The majority voted against the proposition, with 55% voting no to independence.[[109]](#cite_note-114) More powers, particularly concerning taxation, were devolved to the Scottish Parliament after the referendum, following cross-party talks in the [Smith Commission](/wiki/Smith_Commission "Smith Commission").

Since the 2014 referendum, events such as the UK leaving the European Union, despite a majority of voters in Scotland voting to remain a member, have led to calls for a [second independence referendum](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum"). In 2022, the [Lord Advocate](/wiki/Lord_Advocate "Lord Advocate") [Dorothy Bain](/wiki/Dorothy_Bain "Dorothy Bain") argued the case for the Scottish Government to hold another referendum on the issue, with the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") later ruling against the argument.[[110]](#cite_note-115) Following the Supreme Court decision, the Scottish Government stated that it wished to make amendments to the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998") that would allow a referendum to be held.[[111]](#cite_note-116)[[112]](#cite_note-r556-117)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Scotland](/wiki/Geography_of_Scotland "Geography of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Corpach_Basin_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5757851.jpg/250px-Corpach_Basin_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5757851.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corpach_Basin_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5757851.jpg)

At 4,413 feet (1,345 m), [Ben Nevis](/wiki/Ben_Nevis "Ben Nevis") is the highest peak in Scotland and the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles")

The mainland of Scotland comprises the northern third of the land mass of the island of Great Britain, which lies off the northwest coast of [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"). The total area is 30,977 square miles (80,231 km2) with a land area of 30,078 square miles (77,901 km2),[[4]](#cite_note-ONS_Standard_Area_Measurement-6) comparable to the size of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). Scotland's only land border is with England, and runs for 96 miles (154 km) between the basin of the [River Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") on the east coast and the [Solway Firth](/wiki/Solway_Firth "Solway Firth") in the west. The Atlantic Ocean borders the west coast and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") is to the east. The island of Ireland lies only 13 miles (21 km) from the south-western peninsula of [Kintyre](/wiki/Kintyre "Kintyre");[[113]](#cite_note-118) Norway is 190 miles (305 km) to the northeast and the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), 168 miles (270 km) to the north.

The territorial extent of Scotland is generally that established by the 1237 [Treaty of York](/wiki/Treaty_of_York "Treaty of York") between Scotland and the Kingdom of England[[48]](#cite_note-Scotland_Conquered-53) and the 1266 [Treaty of Perth](/wiki/Treaty_of_Perth "Treaty of Perth") between Scotland and Norway.[[16]](#cite_note-Mackie-21) Important exceptions include the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), which having been lost to England in the 14th century is now a [crown dependency](/wiki/Crown_dependency "Crown dependency") outside of the United Kingdom; the island groups [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland"), which were acquired from Norway in 1472;[[114]](#cite_note-Whitaker-119) and [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") (after changing ownership several times) was ceded to England in 1482.[[115]](#cite_note-q088-120)

The geographical [centre of Scotland](/wiki/Centre_of_Scotland "Centre of Scotland") lies a few miles from the village of [Newtonmore](/wiki/Newtonmore "Newtonmore") in [Badenoch](/wiki/Badenoch "Badenoch").[[116]](#cite_note-121) Rising to 4,413 feet (1,345 m) above sea level, Scotland's highest point is the summit of [Ben Nevis](/wiki/Ben_Nevis "Ben Nevis"), in [Lochaber](/wiki/Lochaber "Lochaber"), while Scotland's longest river, the [River Tay](/wiki/River_Tay "River Tay"), flows for a distance of 117 miles (188 km).[[11]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-16)

### Geology and geomorphology

Main article: [Geology of Scotland](/wiki/Geology_of_Scotland "Geology of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Arthur%27s_Seat_seen_from_the_Pentlands_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7138235.jpg/250px-Arthur%27s_Seat_seen_from_the_Pentlands_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7138235.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arthur%27s_Seat_seen_from_the_Pentlands_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7138235.jpg)

[Arthur's Seat](/wiki/Arthur%27s_Seat "Arthur's Seat"), Edinburgh

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Loch_Lomond_2013.JPG/250px-Loch_Lomond_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:Loch_Lomond_2013.JPG)

[Loch Lomond](/wiki/Loch_Lomond "Loch Lomond"), a freshwater [loch](/wiki/Loch "Loch"), often considered the boundary between the Lowlands and Highlands[[117]](#cite_note-weir-122)

The whole of Scotland was covered by ice sheets during the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_ages "Ice ages") and the landscape is much affected by glaciation. From a geological perspective, the country has three main sub-divisions: the Highlands and Islands, the Central Lowlands, and the Southern Uplands.

The [Highlands and Islands](/wiki/Highlands_and_Islands "Highlands and Islands") lie to the north and west of the [Highland Boundary Fault](/wiki/Highland_Boundary_Fault "Highland Boundary Fault"), which runs from [Arran](/wiki/Isle_of_Arran "Isle of Arran") to [Stonehaven](/wiki/Stonehaven "Stonehaven"). This part of Scotland largely comprises ancient rocks from the [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") and [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian"), which were uplifted during the later [Caledonian orogeny](/wiki/Caledonian_orogeny "Caledonian orogeny"). It is interspersed with [igneous](/wiki/Igneous "Igneous") intrusions of a more recent age, remnants of which formed mountain massifs such as the [Cairngorms](/wiki/Cairngorms "Cairngorms") and the [Cuillin](/wiki/Cuillin "Cuillin").[[118]](#cite_note-123) In north-eastern mainland Scotland [weathering](/wiki/Weathering "Weathering") of rock that occurred before the [Last Ice Age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") has shaped much of the landscape.[[119]](#cite_note-Hall1986-124)

A significant exception to the above are the fossil-bearing beds of [Old Red Sandstones](/wiki/Old_Red_Sandstone "Old Red Sandstone") found principally along the [Moray Firth](/wiki/Moray_Firth "Moray Firth") coast. The [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") are generally mountainous and the highest elevations in the British Isles are found here. Scotland has over 790 islands divided into four main groups: [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland"), [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney"), and the [Inner Hebrides](/wiki/Inner_Hebrides "Inner Hebrides") and [Outer Hebrides](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides"). There are numerous bodies of freshwater including [Loch Lomond](/wiki/Loch_Lomond "Loch Lomond") and [Loch Ness](/wiki/Loch_Ness "Loch Ness"). Some parts of the coastline consist of [machair](/wiki/Machair_(geography) "Machair (geography)"), a low-lying dune pasture land.

The [Central Lowlands](/wiki/Central_Lowlands "Central Lowlands") is a [rift valley](/wiki/Rift_valley "Rift valley") mainly comprising [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") formations. Many of these sediments have economic significance, for it is here that the coal and iron-bearing rocks that fuelled Scotland's [industrial revolution](/wiki/Industrial_revolution "Industrial revolution") are found. This area has also experienced intense volcanism, [Arthur's Seat](/wiki/Arthur%27s_Seat "Arthur's Seat") in Edinburgh being the remnant of a once much larger volcano. This area is relatively low-lying, although even here hills such as the [Ochils](/wiki/Ochil_Hills "Ochil Hills") and [Campsie Fells](/wiki/Campsie_Fells "Campsie Fells") are rarely far from view.

The [Southern Uplands](/wiki/Southern_Uplands "Southern Uplands") is a range of hills almost 125 miles (200 km) long, interspersed with broad valleys. They lie south of a second [fault line](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)") (the Southern Uplands fault) that runs from [Girvan](/wiki/Girvan "Girvan") to [Dunbar](/wiki/Dunbar "Dunbar").[[120]](#cite_note-125)[[121]](#cite_note-126)[[122]](#cite_note-Scotland_Today_»_ITKT-127) The geological foundations largely comprise [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") deposits laid down some 400 to 500 million years ago. The high point of the Southern Uplands is [Merrick](/wiki/Merrick,_Galloway "Merrick, Galloway") with an elevation of 843 m (2,766 ft).[[15]](#cite_note-Keay-20)[[123]](#cite_note-128)[[124]](#cite_note-129)[[125]](#cite_note-130) The Southern Uplands is home to Scotland's highest village, [Wanlockhead](/wiki/Wanlockhead "Wanlockhead") (430 m or 1,411 ft above sea level).[[122]](#cite_note-Scotland_Today_»_ITKT-127)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Scotland](/wiki/Climate_of_Scotland "Climate of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Traigh_Bail%2C_Tiree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7983.jpg/250px-Traigh_Bail%2C_Tiree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traigh_Bail,_Tiree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7983.jpg)

[Tiree](/wiki/Tiree "Tiree") in the [Inner Hebrides](/wiki/Inner_Hebrides "Inner Hebrides") is one of the sunniest locations in Scotland.

The climate of most of Scotland is [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") and [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"), and tends to be very changeable. As it is warmed by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") from the Atlantic, it has much milder winters (but cooler, wetter summers) than areas on similar latitudes, such as [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador"), southern [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), the [Moscow region](/wiki/Moscow_region "Moscow region") in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), and the [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka_Peninsula "Kamchatka Peninsula") on the opposite side of [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"). Temperatures are generally lower than in the rest of the UK, with the temperature of −27.2 °C (−17.0 °F) recorded at [Braemar](/wiki/Braemar "Braemar") in the [Grampian Mountains](/wiki/Grampian_Mountains "Grampian Mountains"), on 11 February 1895, the coldest ever recorded anywhere in the UK.[[126]](#cite_note-131) The same temperature was also recorded in Braemar on 10 January 1982 and at [Altnaharra](/wiki/Altnaharra "Altnaharra"), [Highland](/wiki/Highland_(council_area) "Highland (council area)"), on 30 December 1995. Winter maxima average 6 °C (43 °F) in the lowlands, with summer maxima averaging 18 °C (64 °F). The highest temperature recorded was 34.8 °C (94.6 °F) at [Charterhall](/wiki/RAF_Charterhall "RAF Charterhall"), [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") on 19 July 2022.[[127]](#cite_note-132)

The west of Scotland is usually warmer than the east, owing to the influence of Atlantic [ocean currents](/wiki/Ocean_currents "Ocean currents") and the colder surface temperatures of the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). [Tiree](/wiki/Tiree "Tiree"), in the Inner Hebrides, is one of the sunniest places in the country: it had more than 300 hours of sunshine in May 1975.[[128]](#cite_note-Metext-133) Rainfall varies widely across Scotland. The western Highlands of Scotland are the wettest, with annual rainfall in a few places exceeding 3,000 mm (120 in).[[129]](#cite_note-134) In comparison, much of lowland Scotland receives less than 800 mm (31 in) annually.[[130]](#cite_note-Meteast-135) Heavy snowfall is not common in the Lowlands, but becomes more common with altitude. Braemar has an average of 59 snow days per year,[[131]](#cite_note-136) while many coastal areas average fewer than 10 days of lying snow per year.[[130]](#cite_note-Meteast-135)

[Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") is expected to have a significant impact on Scotland; if the world warms by 3 degrees, it could cost up to 8% of the GDP of the United Kingdom by 2070. Between 1990 and 2023, Scotland reduced its [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") emissions by 51.3%. In 2026 it adopted a climate change plan with the goal of becoming carbon neutral by 2045. The plan includes making heating houses carbon-free by 2045, covering 21% of the country by forests by 2032, and restoring [peatlands](/wiki/Peatland "Peatland").[[132]](#cite_note-137)[[133]](#cite_note-138)[[134]](#cite_note-139)

### Flora and fauna

Main articles: [Fauna of Scotland](/wiki/Fauna_of_Scotland "Fauna of Scotland") and [Flora of Scotland](/wiki/Flora_of_Scotland "Flora of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/White-tailed_eagle_in_flight_at_Falkenhof_Schloss_Rosenburg.jpg/250px-White-tailed_eagle_in_flight_at_Falkenhof_Schloss_Rosenburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:White-tailed_eagle_in_flight_at_Falkenhof_Schloss_Rosenburg.jpg)

[White-tailed sea eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_sea_eagle "White-tailed sea eagle")

Scotland's wildlife is typical of the north-west of Europe, although several of the larger mammals such as the lynx, brown bear, wolf, elk and walrus were hunted to extinction in historic times. There are important populations of seals and internationally significant nesting grounds for a variety of seabirds such as [gannets](/wiki/Northern_gannet "Northern gannet").[[135]](#cite_note-140) The [golden eagle](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle") is something of a national icon.[[136]](#cite_note-141)

On the high mountain tops, species including [ptarmigan](/wiki/Rock_ptarmigan "Rock ptarmigan"), [mountain hare](/wiki/Mountain_hare "Mountain hare") and [stoat](/wiki/Stoat "Stoat") can be seen in their white colour phase during winter months.[[137]](#cite_note-142) Remnants of the native [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine") forest exist[[138]](#cite_note-143) and within these areas the [Scottish crossbill](/wiki/Scottish_crossbill "Scottish crossbill"), the UK's only [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") bird species and [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate"), can be found alongside [capercaillie](/wiki/Western_capercaillie "Western capercaillie"), [Scottish wildcat](/wiki/Scottish_wildcat "Scottish wildcat"), [red squirrel](/wiki/Red_squirrel "Red squirrel") and [pine marten](/wiki/Pine_marten "Pine marten").[[139]](#cite_note-144)[[140]](#cite_note-145)[[141]](#cite_note-146) Various animals have been re-introduced, including the [white-tailed eagle](/wiki/White-tailed_eagle "White-tailed eagle") in 1975, the [red kite](/wiki/Red_kite "Red kite") in the 1980s,[[142]](#cite_note-147)[[143]](#cite_note-148) and there have been experimental projects involving the [beaver](/wiki/European_beaver "European beaver") and [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), which are both now relatively widespread. Today, much of the remaining native [Caledonian Forest](/wiki/Caledonian_Forest "Caledonian Forest") lies within the [Cairngorms National Park](/wiki/Cairngorms_National_Park "Cairngorms National Park") and remnants of the forest remain at 84 locations across Scotland. On the west coast, remnants of ancient Celtic Rainforest remain, particularly on the Taynish peninsula in [Argyll](/wiki/Argyll "Argyll"), these forests are particularly rare due to high rates of deforestation throughout Scottish history.[[144]](#cite_note-149)[[145]](#cite_note-BBC_News-150) Scotland now ranks in the lowest 15% of countries in the Biodiversity Intactness Index.[[146]](#cite_note-151)[[147]](#cite_note-152)

The flora of the country is varied incorporating both [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") and [coniferous](/wiki/Coniferous "Coniferous") woodland as well as [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") and [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") species. Large-scale commercial tree planting and management of upland moorland habitat for the grazing of sheep and [field sport](/wiki/Field_sports "Field sports") activities like [deer stalking](/wiki/Deer_stalking "Deer stalking") and [driven grouse shooting](/wiki/Driven_grouse_shooting "Driven grouse shooting") impacts the distribution of [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_(ecology) "Indigenous (ecology)") plants and animals.[[148]](#cite_note-153) The UK's tallest tree is a [grand fir](/wiki/Abies_grandis "Abies grandis") planted beside [Loch Fyne](/wiki/Loch_Fyne "Loch Fyne"), Argyll in the 1870s, and the [Fortingall Yew](/wiki/Fortingall_Yew "Fortingall Yew") may be 5,000 years old and is probably the oldest living thing in Europe.[[149]](#cite_note-154)[[150]](#cite_note-155)[[151]](#cite_note-156) Although the number of native [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") is low by world standards, Scotland's substantial [bryophyte](/wiki/Bryophyte "Bryophyte") flora is of global importance.[[152]](#cite_note-157)[[153]](#cite_note-RBGE-158)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Scotland](/wiki/Politics_of_Scotland "Politics of Scotland"), [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), and [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government")

Further information: [Political parties in Scotland](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Scotland "Political parties in Scotland"), [Elections in Scotland](/wiki/Elections_in_Scotland "Elections in Scotland"), and [Electoral system of Scotland](/wiki/Electoral_system_of_Scotland "Electoral system of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg/250px-King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_III_(July_2023).jpg)

[King Charles III](/wiki/King_Charles_III "King Charles III")  
[Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom")  
since 2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/John_Swinney_-_First_Minister_%2853720492021%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-John_Swinney_-_First_Minister_%2853720492021%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Swinney_-_First_Minister_(53720492021)_(cropped).jpg)

[John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney")  
[First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland")  
since 2024

Scotland is part of the United Kingdom, a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") whose current sovereign is [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III").[[154]](#cite_note-159) The monarchy uses a variety of styles, titles and other symbols specific to Scotland, most of which originated in the pre–union [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"). These include the [Royal Standard of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_Scotland "Royal Standard of Scotland"), the [royal coat of arms](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom#In_Scotland "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom"), and the title [Duke of Rothesay](/wiki/Duke_of_Rothesay "Duke of Rothesay"), which is traditionally given to the heir apparent. There are also distinct Scottish [Officers of State](/wiki/Great_Officers_of_State_(United_Kingdom)#Scotland "Great Officers of State (United Kingdom)") and Officers of the Crown, and the [Order of the Thistle](/wiki/Order_of_the_Thistle "Order of the Thistle"), a [chivalric order](/wiki/Order_of_chivalry "Order of chivalry"), is specific to the country.[[155]](#cite_note-160)

The [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") are the country's primary legislative bodies. The UK Parliament is [sovereign](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty "Parliamentary sovereignty") and therefore has supremacy over the Scottish Parliament,[[156]](#cite_note-161) but generally restricts itself to legislating over reserved matters: primarily some taxes, some aspects of social security, defence, international relations, and broadcasting.[[157]](#cite_note-Gate22-162) There is a [convention](/wiki/Legislative_consent_motion "Legislative consent motion") the UK Parliament will not legislate over devolved matters without the Scottish Parliament's consent.[[158]](#cite_note-163) Scotland is represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), the [lower chamber](/wiki/Lower_chamber "Lower chamber") of the UK Parliament, by 57 [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (out of a total of 650).[[159]](#cite_note-164) They are elected to single-member [constituencies](/wiki/Scottish_Westminster_constituencies "Scottish Westminster constituencies") under the [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") system of voting. The [Scotland Office](/wiki/Scotland_Office "Scotland Office") represents the British government in Scotland and represents Scottish interests within the government.[[160]](#cite_note-165) The Scotland Office is led by the [secretary of state for Scotland](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Scotland "Secretary of State for Scotland"), who sits in the [Cabinet of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom").[[161]](#cite_note-jack2-166) The Labour MP [Douglas Alexander](/wiki/Douglas_Alexander "Douglas Alexander") has held the position since September 2025,[[162]](#cite_note-167) replacing the [Ian Murray](/wiki/Ian_Murray_(Scottish_politician) "Ian Murray (Scottish politician)"), who had served from July 2024.[[163]](#cite_note-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Scottish_Parliament_Debating_Chamber_1.jpg/250px-Scottish_Parliament_Debating_Chamber_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_Parliament_Debating_Chamber_1.jpg)

Debating chamber of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") in Edinburgh

The Scottish Parliament is a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") legislature with 129 members (MSPs): 73 of them represent individual constituencies and are elected on a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") system, and the other 56 are elected in eight different electoral regions by the [additional member system](/wiki/Additional_member_system_(Scottish_Parliament) "Additional member system (Scottish Parliament)"). MSPs normally serve for a five-year period.[[164]](#cite_note-169) The Scottish Parliament has the most extensive [devolved powers](/wiki/Devolved,_reserved_and_excepted_matters "Devolved, reserved and excepted matters") amongst the [devolved parliaments](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") in the United Kingdom,[[165]](#cite_note-170) and considered one of the most powerful devolved parliaments internationally.[[166]](#cite_note-171) The largest party since the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election"), has been the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP), which won 64 of the 129 seats.[[167]](#cite_note-2016_results-172) The [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives"), [Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour"), the [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") and the [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens") also have representation in the current Parliament.[[167]](#cite_note-2016_results-172) The [next Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/Next_Scottish_Parliament_election "Next Scottish Parliament election") is due to be held on 7 May 2026.[[168]](#cite_note-173)
