The [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), which has been described as one of the most powerful devolved governments globally,[[169]](#cite_note-174) is led by the [first minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), who is nominated by MSPs and is typically the leader of the largest party in the Parliament. Other ministers are appointed by the first minister and serve at their discretion.[[170]](#cite_note-175) As the head of the Scottish Government, the first minister is responsible for the comprehensive development, implementation and presentation of government policy, and is responsible for promoting the interests of the country at home and internationally.[[171]](#cite_note-176) [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney"), leader of the Scottish National Party (SNP), has served as the first minister since 8 May 2024.[[172]](#cite_note-177)

### Diplomacy and relations

Main articles: [International relations of Scotland](/wiki/International_relations_of_Scotland "International relations of Scotland") and [List of consular missions in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_consular_missions_in_Scotland "List of consular missions in Scotland")

Further information: [Intergovernmental relations in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Intergovernmental_relations_in_the_United_Kingdom "Intergovernmental relations in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/President_Donald_Trump_meets_with_Scottish_First_Minister_John_Swinney_and_U.K._Ambassador_to_the_U.S._Peter_Mandelson_%2854778210513%29.jpg/250px-President_Donald_Trump_meets_with_Scottish_First_Minister_John_Swinney_and_U.K._Ambassador_to_the_U.S._Peter_Mandelson_%2854778210513%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Donald_Trump_meets_with_Scottish_First_Minister_John_Swinney_and_U.K._Ambassador_to_the_U.S._Peter_Mandelson_(54778210513).jpg)

First Minister Swinney meets with U.S. [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), September 2025

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Bilateral_meeting_with_Icelandic_Prime_Minister_%2832798372587%29.jpg/250px-Bilateral_meeting_with_Icelandic_Prime_Minister_%2832798372587%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bilateral_meeting_with_Icelandic_Prime_Minister_(32798372587).jpg)

First Minister Sturgeon holds a bilateral meeting with Icelandic [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Iceland "Prime Minister of Iceland") [Katrín Jakobsdóttir](/wiki/Katr%C3%ADn_Jakobsd%C3%B3ttir "Katrín Jakobsdóttir"), April 2019

As leader of the Scottish Government, the first minister is a member of the [Prime Minister and Heads of Devolved Governments Council](/wiki/Prime_Minister_and_Heads_of_Devolved_Governments_Council "Prime Minister and Heads of Devolved Governments Council") and the [Council of the Nations and Regions](/wiki/Council_of_the_Nations_and_Regions "Council of the Nations and Regions"), the bodies which facilitate intergovernmental relations within the United Kingdom.[[173]](#cite_note-178) Foreign policy is a reserved matter and primarily the responsibility of the [Foreign Office](/wiki/Foreign,_Commonwealth_and_Development_Office "Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office"), a department of the UK Government.[[174]](#cite_note-179) Nevertheless, the Scottish Government may promote Scottish interests abroad and encourage foreign investment in Scotland.[[175]](#cite_note-180) The first minister and the [constitution secretary](/wiki/Constitution_secretary "Constitution secretary") have portfolios which include foreign affairs.[[176]](#cite_note-181)[[177]](#cite_note-182)[[178]](#cite_note-assets.publishing.service.gov.uk-183) Scotland's [international network](/wiki/International_relations_of_Scotland "International relations of Scotland") consists of two Scotland Houses, one in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") and the other in [London](/wiki/London "London"), seven Scottish Government international offices, and over thirty Scottish Development International offices in other countries globally. Both Scotland Houses are independent Scottish Government establishments, whilst the seven Scottish Government international offices are based in [British embassies](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of diplomatic missions of the United Kingdom") or British High Commission offices.[[179]](#cite_note-report-2022-23-184) The Scottish Government has a network of offices in Beijing, Berlin, Brussels, Copenhagen, Dublin, London, Ottawa, Paris, and Washington, D.C., which promote Scottish interests in their respective areas.[[180]](#cite_note-185) Additionally, the country has a number of [foreign diplomatic missions](/wiki/List_of_consular_missions_in_Scotland "List of consular missions in Scotland"), most of which are located in the capital city, Edinburgh.[[181]](#cite_note-186)

The nation has historic ties to France as a result of the '[Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance")', a treaty signed between the Kingdom of Scotland and [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") in 1295 to discourage an English invasion of either country.[[182]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-187) The alliance effectively ended in the sixteenth century, but the two countries continue to have a close relationship, with a Statement of Intent being signed in 2013 between the Scottish Government and the [Government of France](/wiki/Government_of_France "Government of France").[[183]](#cite_note-188) In 2004 the [Scotland Malawi Partnership](/wiki/Scotland_Malawi_Partnership "Scotland Malawi Partnership") was established, which co-ordinates Scottish activities to strengthen existing links with [Malawi](/wiki/Malawi "Malawi"), and in 2021, the Scottish Government and [Government of Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland") signed the Ireland-Scotland Bilateral Review, committing both governments to increased levels of co-operation on areas such as diplomacy, economy and business.[[179]](#cite_note-report-2022-23-184)[[184]](#cite_note-189)[[185]](#cite_note-firstminister.gov.scot-190) Scotland also has [historical and cultural](/wiki/Scandinavian_Scotland "Scandinavian Scotland") ties with the [Scandinavian countries](/wiki/Scandinavian_countries "Scandinavian countries").[[186]](#cite_note-191)[[187]](#cite_note-192) Scottish Government policy advocates for stronger political relations with the [Nordic](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") and [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") countries, which has resulted in some Nordic-inspired policies being adopted such as baby boxes.[[188]](#cite_note-:11-193)[[189]](#cite_note-194) Representatives from the Scottish Parliament attended the [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council") for the first time in 2022.[[190]](#cite_note-195)

Scotland is a member of the [British–Irish Council](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Council "British–Irish Council"), the [Conference of European Regions with Legislative Power](/wiki/REGLEG "REGLEG") (REGLEG), the [British–Irish Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Parliamentary_Assembly "British–Irish Parliamentary Assembly"), the [Council of European Municipalities and Regions](/wiki/Council_of_European_Municipalities_and_Regions "Council of European Municipalities and Regions"),[[191]](#cite_note-196) the [Inter-Parliamentary Forum](/wiki/Inter-Parliamentary_Forum "Inter-Parliamentary Forum"), the [Congress of Local and Regional Authorities](/wiki/Congress_of_Local_and_Regional_Authorities "Congress of Local and Regional Authorities"),[[192]](#cite_note-197) the [Commonwealth Parliamentary Association](/wiki/Commonwealth_Parliamentary_Association "Commonwealth Parliamentary Association")[[193]](#cite_note-198)[[194]](#cite_note-199)[[195]](#cite_note-200) and the [Parliamentary Partnership Assembly with the European Union](/wiki/EU%E2%80%93UK_Parliamentary_Partnership_Assembly "EU–UK Parliamentary Partnership Assembly").[[196]](#cite_note-201) Scotland held the Presidency of Conference of European Regions with Legislative Power from November 2003 until November 2004 during the [premiership of Jack McConnell](/wiki/Premiership_of_Jack_McConnell "Premiership of Jack McConnell").[[197]](#cite_note-202)

### Devolution and independence

Main articles: [Scottish devolution](/wiki/Scottish_devolution "Scottish devolution") and [Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Donald_Dewar_Statue%2C_Glasgow_Royal_Concert_Hall_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Donald_Dewar_Statue%2C_Glasgow_Royal_Concert_Hall_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Dewar_Statue,_Glasgow_Royal_Concert_Hall_(cropped).jpg)

[Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar"), the inaugural [first minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") and was referred to as the "[Father of the Nation](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation")"[[198]](#cite_note-203)

[Devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution")—the granting of central government powers to a regional government[[199]](#cite_note-204)– gained increasing popularity as a policy in the United Kingdom the late twentieth century; it was described by [John Smith](/wiki/John_Smith_(Labour_Party_leader) "John Smith (Labour Party leader)"), then [Leader of the Labour Party](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Labour_Party_(UK) "Leader of the Labour Party (UK)"), as the "settled will of the Scottish people".[[200]](#cite_note-205) The Scottish Parliament and [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") were subsequently established under the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998"); the Act followed a successful [referendum in 1997](/wiki/Scottish_devolution_referendum,_1997 "Scottish devolution referendum, 1997") which found majority support for both creating the Parliament and granting it limited powers to [vary income tax](/wiki/Scottish_variable_rate "Scottish variable rate").[[201]](#cite_note-206) The Act enabled the new institutions to legislate in all areas not explicitly reserved by the UK Parliament.[[202]](#cite_note-Devolved_and_Reserved_Powers-207)

Two more pieces of legislation, the Scotland Acts of [2012](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2012 "Scotland Act 2012") and [2016](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2016 "Scotland Act 2016"), gave the Scottish Parliament further powers to legislate on taxation and social security;[[203]](#cite_note-208) the 2016 Act also gave the Scottish Government powers to manage the affairs of the [Crown Estate in Scotland](/wiki/Crown_Estate_Scotland "Crown Estate Scotland").[[204]](#cite_note-209) Conversely, the [United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Internal_Market_Act_2020 "United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020") constrains the Scottish Parliament's autonomy to regulate goods and services,[[205]](#cite_note-MaMu22-210)[[206]](#cite_note-DouHu22-211) and the academic view is that this undermines devolution.[[212]](#cite_note-217)

The [2007 Scottish Parliament elections](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_general_election,_2007 "Scottish Parliament general election, 2007") led to the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP), which supports [Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence"), forming a [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government"). The new government established a "[National Conversation](/wiki/National_Conversation "National Conversation")" on constitutional issues, proposing a number of options such as increasing the powers of the Scottish Parliament, [federalism](/wiki/Federation "Federation"), or a referendum on Scottish independence from the United Kingdom. The three main unionist opposition parties–[Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour"), the [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives"), and the [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats")–created a separate [commission](/wiki/Calman_Commission "Calman Commission") to investigate the distribution of powers between devolved Scottish and UK-wide bodies while not considering independence.[[213]](#cite_note-218) In August 2009 the SNP proposed a bill to hold a referendum on independence in November 2010, but was defeated by opposition from all other major parties.[[214]](#cite_note-ReferendumBill2010-219)[[215]](#cite_note-Times3Sep09-220)[[216]](#cite_note-221)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Visit_of_Nicola_Sturgeon%2C_First_Minister_of_the_Scottish_Government%2C_to_the_EC.jpg/250px-Visit_of_Nicola_Sturgeon%2C_First_Minister_of_the_Scottish_Government%2C_to_the_EC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Visit_of_Nicola_Sturgeon,_First_Minister_of_the_Scottish_Government,_to_the_EC.jpg)

First Minister [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon") with [Michel Barnier](/wiki/Michel_Barnier "Michel Barnier") in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), to discuss Scottish affairs following [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), 2019

The [2011 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election") resulted in an SNP overall majority in the Scottish Parliament, and on 18 September 2014 a [referendum on Scottish independence](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") was held.[[217]](#cite_note-222) The referendum resulted in a rejection of independence, by 55.3% to 44.7%.[[218]](#cite_note-223)[[219]](#cite_note-224) During the campaign, the three main parties in the British Parliament–the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), and the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")–pledged to extend the powers of the Scottish Parliament.[[220]](#cite_note-225)[[221]](#cite_note-kelvin-226) An all-party [commission](/wiki/Smith_Commission "Smith Commission") chaired by [Robert Smith, Baron Smith of Kelvin](/wiki/Robert_Smith,_Baron_Smith_of_Kelvin "Robert Smith, Baron Smith of Kelvin") was formed,[[221]](#cite_note-kelvin-226) which led to the Scotland Act 2016.[[222]](#cite_note-227)

Following the [European Union Referendum Act 2015](/wiki/European_Union_Referendum_Act_2015 "European Union Referendum Act 2015"), the [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") was held on 23 June 2016 on Britain's membership of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). A majority in the United Kingdom voted to withdraw from the EU, while a majority within Scotland voted to remain a member.[[223]](#cite_note-:2-228) The first minister, Nicola Sturgeon, announced the following day that as a result a [new independence referendum](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum") was "highly likely".[[224]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-:2-228) On 31 January 2020, the United Kingdom formally withdrew from the European Union. Because constitutional affairs are reserved matters under the Scotland Act, the Scottish Parliament would again have to be granted temporary additional powers under Section 30 to hold a legally binding vote.[[225]](#cite_note-:3-230)[[226]](#cite_note-231)[[227]](#cite_note-232)

### Local government

Main articles: [Local government in Scotland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland") and [Subdivisions of Scotland](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Scotland "Subdivisions of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Cunningham_House%2C_Irvine.jpg/250px-Cunningham_House%2C_Irvine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cunningham_House,_Irvine.jpg)

Cunninghame House in [Irvine, North Ayrshire](/wiki/Irvine,_North_Ayrshire "Irvine, North Ayrshire"), seat of [North Ayrshire Council](/wiki/North_Ayrshire_Council "North Ayrshire Council")

For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland") purposes Scotland is subdivided into 32 single-tier council areas.[[228]](#cite_note-233) The areas were established in 1996, and their councils are responsible for the provision of all local government services. Decisions are made by councillors, who are elected at [local elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Scotland "Elections in Scotland") every five years. The leader of the council is typically a councillor from the party with the most seats; councils also have a civic head, typically called the [provost](/wiki/Provost_(civil) "Provost (civil)") or [lord provost](/wiki/Lord_provost "Lord provost"), who represents the council on ceremonial occasions and chairs council meetings.[[229]](#cite_note-234) [Community Councils](/wiki/Community_council "Community council") are informal organisations that represent smaller subdivisions within each council area.[[230]](#cite_note-:9-235)

[Police Scotland](/wiki/Police_Scotland "Police Scotland") and the [Scottish Fire and Rescue Service](/wiki/Scottish_Fire_and_Rescue_Service "Scottish Fire and Rescue Service") cover the entire country. For healthcare and postal districts, and a number of other governmental and non-governmental organisations such as the churches, there are other long-standing methods of subdividing Scotland for the purposes of administration.

There are [eight cities in Scotland](/wiki/Cities_of_Scotland "Cities of Scotland"): [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee"), [Dunfermline](/wiki/Dunfermline "Dunfermline"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), [Inverness](/wiki/Inverness "Inverness"), [Perth](/wiki/Perth,_Scotland "Perth, Scotland") and [Stirling](/wiki/Stirling "Stirling").[[231]](#cite_note-236) [City status in the United Kingdom](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") is conferred by the monarch through [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent").[[232]](#cite_note-237)

### Military

Main article: [Armed forces in Scotland](/wiki/Armed_forces_in_Scotland "Armed forces in Scotland")

Further information: [Scots Army](/wiki/Scots_Army "Scots Army"), [Royal Scots Navy](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Navy "Royal Scots Navy"), and [Military history of Scotland](/wiki/Military_history_of_Scotland "Military history of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Eurofighter_Typhoon%2C_ZK312_%2819451923880%29.jpg/250px-Eurofighter_Typhoon%2C_ZK312_%2819451923880%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eurofighter_Typhoon,_ZK312_(19451923880).jpg)

A [Typhoon FGR4](/wiki/Eurofighter_Typhoon "Eurofighter Typhoon") in [No. 6 Squadron](/wiki/No._6_Squadron_RAF "No. 6 Squadron RAF") markings at [RAF Lossiemouth](/wiki/RAF_Lossiemouth "RAF Lossiemouth")

As one of the countries of the United Kingdom, the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") are the armed forces of Scotland. Of the money spent on UK defence, about £3.3 billion could be attributed to Scotland in 2018–2019.[[233]](#cite_note-238) Scotland had a long military tradition predating the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") with England. Following the Treaty of Union in 1707, the [Scots Army](/wiki/Scots_Army "Scots Army") and [Royal Scots Navy](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Navy "Royal Scots Navy") merged with their English counterparts to form the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), which together form part of the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces").[[234]](#cite_note-239)[[235]](#cite_note-240) The [Atholl Highlanders](/wiki/Atholl_Highlanders "Atholl Highlanders"), Europe's only remaining legal [private army](/wiki/Private_army "Private army"), did not join the Scots Army or Royal Scots Navy in merging with English armed forces, remaining a private army not under the command of the British Armed Forces.[[236]](#cite_note-241)

Numerous [Scottish regiments](/wiki/Scottish_regiment "Scottish regiment") have at various times existed in the British Army. Distinctively Scottish regiments in the British Army include the [Scots Guards](/wiki/Scots_Guards "Scots Guards"), the [Royal Scots Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Dragoon_Guards "Royal Scots Dragoon Guards") and the [154 (Scottish) Regiment RLC](/wiki/154_(Scottish)_Regiment_RLC "154 (Scottish) Regiment RLC"), an [Army Reserve](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)") regiment of the [Royal Logistic Corps](/wiki/Royal_Logistic_Corps "Royal Logistic Corps"). In 2006, as a result of the *[Delivering Security in a Changing World](/wiki/Delivering_Security_in_a_Changing_World "Delivering Security in a Changing World")* [white paper](/wiki/White_paper "White paper"), the Scottish infantry regiments in the [Scottish Division](/wiki/Scottish_Division "Scottish Division") were amalgamated to form the [Royal Regiment of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Scotland "Royal Regiment of Scotland").[[237]](#cite_note-242) As a result of the [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition")'s [Strategic Defence and Security Review 2010](/wiki/Strategic_Defence_and_Security_Review_2010 "Strategic Defence and Security Review 2010"), the Scottish regiments of the line in the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") [infantry](/wiki/Infantry_of_the_British_Army "Infantry of the British Army"), having previously formed the Scottish Division, were reorganised into the [Scottish, Welsh and Irish Division](/wiki/Scottish,_Welsh_and_Irish_Division "Scottish, Welsh and Irish Division") in 2017. Before the formation of the Scottish Division, the Scottish infantry was organised into a [Lowland Brigade](/wiki/Lowland_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "Lowland Brigade (United Kingdom)") and [Highland Brigade](/wiki/Highland_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "Highland Brigade (United Kingdom)").[[238]](#cite_note-243)

Because of their topography and perceived remoteness, parts of Scotland have housed many sensitive defence establishments.[[239]](#cite_note-244)[[240]](#cite_note-245)[[241]](#cite_note-246) Between 1960 and 1991, the [Holy Loch](/wiki/Holy_Loch "Holy Loch") was a base for the US fleet of [Polaris](/wiki/Polaris_ballistic_missile "Polaris ballistic missile")-equipped [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine").[[242]](#cite_note-247) Today, [His Majesty's Naval Base Clyde](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde"), 25 miles (40 kilometres) north-west of Glasgow, is the base for the four [Trident](/wiki/Trident_(missile) "Trident (missile)")-armed [*Vanguard*-class](/wiki/Vanguard-class_submarine "Vanguard-class submarine") [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") that comprise the Britain's [nuclear deterrent](/wiki/Nuclear_deterrent "Nuclear deterrent").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Challenger2-Bergen-Hohne-Training-Area-2.jpg/250px-Challenger2-Bergen-Hohne-Training-Area-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Challenger2-Bergen-Hohne-Training-Area-2.jpg)

A [Challenger 2](/wiki/Challenger_2 "Challenger 2") [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") of the [Royal Scots Dragoon Guards](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Dragoon_Guards "Royal Scots Dragoon Guards")

Scotland's [Scapa Flow](/wiki/Scapa_Flow "Scapa Flow") was the main base for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") in the 20th century.[[243]](#cite_note-248) As the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") intensified in 1961, the United States deployed [Polaris ballistic missiles](/wiki/Polaris_ballistic_missile "Polaris ballistic missile"), and submarines, in the [Firth of Clyde](/wiki/Firth_of_Clyde "Firth of Clyde")'s [Holy Loch](/wiki/Holy_Loch "Holy Loch"). Public protests from [CND](/wiki/CND "CND") campaigners proved futile. The Royal Navy successfully convinced the government to allow the base because it wanted its own Polaris submarines, and it obtained them in 1963. The RN's nuclear submarine base opened with four [*Resolution*-class](/wiki/Resolution-class_submarine "Resolution-class submarine") Polaris submarines at the expanded [Faslane Naval Base](/wiki/HMNB_Clyde "HMNB Clyde") on the [Gare Loch](/wiki/Gare_Loch "Gare Loch"). The first patrol of a [Trident](/wiki/Trident_missile "Trident missile")-armed submarine occurred in 1994, although the US base was closed at the end of the Cold War.[[244]](#cite_note-249)

A single front-line [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") base is located in Scotland. [RAF Lossiemouth](/wiki/RAF_Lossiemouth "RAF Lossiemouth"), located in [Moray](/wiki/Moray "Moray"), is the most northerly [air defence](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") [fighter](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft") base in the United Kingdom and is home to four [Eurofighter Typhoon](/wiki/Eurofighter_Typhoon "Eurofighter Typhoon") combat aircraft squadrons, three [Poseidon MRA1](/wiki/Poseidon_MRA1 "Poseidon MRA1") squadrons, and a full–time, permanently based RAF Regiment squadron.[[245]](#cite_note-250) An additional four RAF Reserve Squadrons are based in Scotland – 612 Squadron, 2622 (Highland) Squadron and 602 Squadron in Glasgow, and 603 Squadron in Edinburgh.[[246]](#cite_note-251)

### Law and order

Main article: [Scots law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Parliament_Square%2C_Edinburgh_facing_east.jpg/250px-Parliament_Square%2C_Edinburgh_facing_east.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parliament_Square,_Edinburgh_facing_east.jpg)

[Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh") in Edinburgh, the former [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"), now houses the [Supreme Courts of Scotland](/wiki/College_of_Justice "College of Justice")

Scots law has a basis derived from [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"),[[247]](#cite_note-252) combining features of both uncodified [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"), dating back to the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")*, and [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") with [medieval sources](/wiki/Legal_institutions_of_Scotland_in_the_High_Middle_Ages "Legal institutions of Scotland in the High Middle Ages"). The terms of the Treaty of Union with England in 1707 guaranteed the continued existence of a separate legal system in Scotland from that of England and Wales.[[248]](#cite_note-253) Prior to 1611, there were several regional law systems in Scotland, most notably [Udal law](/wiki/Udal_law "Udal law") in [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland"), based on old Norse law. Various other systems derived from common [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_law "Celtic law") or [Brehon laws](/wiki/Brehon_laws "Brehon laws") survived in the Highlands until the 1800s.[[249]](#cite_note-254)
Scots law provides for three types of [courts](/wiki/Courts_of_Scotland "Courts of Scotland") responsible for the administration of justice: civil, criminal and [heraldic](/wiki/Law_of_Arms "Law of Arms"). The supreme civil court is the [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session"), although civil [appeals](/wiki/Appeal_(law) "Appeal (law)") can be taken to the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") (or before 1 October 2009, the [House of Lords](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords")). The [High Court of Justiciary](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justiciary "High Court of Justiciary") is the supreme criminal court in Scotland. The Court of Session is housed at [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh"), in Edinburgh, which was the home of the pre-Union [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") with the High Court of Justiciary and the Supreme Court of Appeal currently located at the [Lawnmarket](/wiki/Lawnmarket "Lawnmarket"). The [sheriff court](/wiki/Sheriff_court "Sheriff court") is the main criminal and civil court, hearing most cases. There are 49 sheriff courts throughout the country.[[250]](#cite_note-255) [District courts](/wiki/District_Courts_of_Scotland "District Courts of Scotland") were introduced in 1975 for minor offences and small claims. These were gradually replaced by [Justice of the Peace Courts](/wiki/Justice_of_the_Peace_Court "Justice of the Peace Court") from 2008 to 2010.

For three centuries the Scots legal system was unique for being the only national legal system without a parliament. This ended with the advent of the Scottish Parliament in 1999, which legislates for devolved matters.[[202]](#cite_note-Devolved_and_Reserved_Powers-207) Many features within the system have been preserved. Within criminal law, the Scots legal system is unique in having three possible verdicts: "guilty", "not guilty" and "*[not proven](/wiki/Not_proven "Not proven")*".[[251]](#cite_note-256) Both "not guilty" and "not proven" result in an [acquittal](/wiki/Acquittal "Acquittal"), typically with no possibility of retrial per the rule of [double jeopardy](/wiki/Double_jeopardy "Double jeopardy"). A retrial can hear new evidence at a later date that might have proven conclusive in the earlier trial at first instance, where the person acquitted subsequently admits the offence or where it can be proved that the acquittal was tainted by an attempt to [pervert the course of justice](/wiki/Perverting_the_course_of_justice "Perverting the course of justice"). Scots juries, sitting in criminal cases, consist of fifteen jurors, which is three more than is typical in many countries.[[252]](#cite_note-257)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Court_of_Session_%2853707036026%29.jpg/250px-Court_of_Session_%2853707036026%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Court_of_Session_(53707036026).jpg)

The [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session") is the highest national court for [civil cases](/wiki/Civil_law_(common_law) "Civil law (common law)")

The [Lord Advocate](/wiki/Lord_Advocate "Lord Advocate") is the chief legal officer of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") and [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") in Scotland. The Lord Advocate is the head of the systems in Scotland for the investigation and prosecution of crime, the investigation of deaths as well as serving as the principal legal adviser to the Scottish Government and representing the government in legal proceedings.[[253]](#cite_note-auto1-258) They are the chief [public prosecutor](/wiki/Public_prosecutor "Public prosecutor") for Scotland and all [prosecutions](/wiki/Prosecution "Prosecution") on indictment are conducted by the [Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service](/wiki/Crown_Office_and_Procurator_Fiscal_Service "Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service") in the Lord Advocate's name on behalf of the Monarch.[[253]](#cite_note-auto1-258) The officeholder is one of the [Great Officers of State](/wiki/Great_Officers_of_State_(United_Kingdom)#Scotland "Great Officers of State (United Kingdom)") of Scotland. The current Lord Advocate, [Dorothy Bain](/wiki/Dorothy_Bain "Dorothy Bain"), was nominated by first minister Nicola Sturgeon, and was appointed in June 2021.[[254]](#cite_note-259) The Lord Advocate is supported by the [Solicitor General for Scotland](/wiki/Solicitor_General_for_Scotland "Solicitor General for Scotland").[[255]](#cite_note-260)

Since 2013, Scotland has had a unified police force known as [Police Scotland](/wiki/Police_Scotland "Police Scotland"). The [Scottish Prison Service](/wiki/Scottish_Prison_Service "Scottish Prison Service") (SPS) manages the prisons in Scotland, which collectively house over 8,500 prisoners.[[256]](#cite_note-261) The [Cabinet Secretary for Justice and Home Affairs](/wiki/Cabinet_Secretary_for_Justice_and_Home_Affairs "Cabinet Secretary for Justice and Home Affairs") is responsible for the Scottish Prison Service within the Scottish Government.

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Scotland](/wiki/Economy_of_Scotland "Economy of Scotland")

Further information: [Economic history of Scotland](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Scotland "Economic history of Scotland"), [List of companies of Scotland](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Scotland "List of companies of Scotland"), [Taxation in Scotland](/wiki/Taxation_in_Scotland "Taxation in Scotland"), and [Scottish budget](/wiki/Scottish_budget "Scottish budget")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Skyline_of_Edinburgh.jpg/250px-Skyline_of_Edinburgh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skyline_of_Edinburgh.jpg)

[Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), the 13th-largest financial centre in the world and 4th largest in Europe in 2020[[257]](#cite_note-262)

Scotland has a Western-style [open](/wiki/Open_economy "Open economy") [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy"), considered one of the leading [financial centres](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") in Europe, and is the largest financial centre in the United Kingdom outside of London.[[258]](#cite_note-263) Edinburgh is the financial services centre of Scotland, with large finance firms based there including [Lloyds Banking Group](/wiki/Lloyds_Banking_Group "Lloyds Banking Group"), the [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland"), the Government-owned [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") and [Standard Life](/wiki/Standard_Life "Standard Life").[[259]](#cite_note-264) Edinburgh was ranked 15th in the [list of world financial centres](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") in 2007,[[260]](#cite_note-265) and in 2024, was ranked 33rd internationally and 10th across Europe.[[261]](#cite_note-Scottish_cities_rise_in_Global_Financial_Centres_Index_as_industry_reports_13,000_new_jobs-266) Glasgow, Scotland's largest city, also ranks as a significant financial centre in the country, ranked 42nd internationally in 2024 and is widely regarded as an established economic player with particular strengths in domestic expertise.[[261]](#cite_note-Scottish_cities_rise_in_Global_Financial_Centres_Index_as_industry_reports_13,000_new_jobs-266) Scotland is ranked within the top 10 of the largest European regions for foreign direct investment, and is rated as one "of the most promising investment destinations in Europe".[[262]](#cite_note-267)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Distillery-gates.jpg/250px-Distillery-gates.jpg)](/wiki/File:Distillery-gates.jpg)

[Scotch whisky](/wiki/Scotch_whisky "Scotch whisky") production at [Bruichladdich distillery](/wiki/Bruichladdich_distillery "Bruichladdich distillery")

In 2024, Scotland's gross domestic product (GDP), including offshore oil and gas, was estimated at £223.4 billion.[[8]](#cite_note-Scot_Gov_GDP-10) In 2021, Scottish exports in goods and services (excluding intra-UK trade) were estimated to be £50.1 billion.[[263]](#cite_note-ONS_international_trade-268) Scotland's primary goods exports are mineral fuels, machinery and transport, and beverages and tobacco.[[264]](#cite_note-HMRC_goods_trade-269) The country's largest export markets in goods are the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") (£6.9 billion), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (£4.0 billion), the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") (£2.8 billion), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (£2.8 billion), [France](/wiki/France "France") (£2.3 billion) and [China](/wiki/China "China") (£0.8 billion).[[265]](#cite_note-270) Its largest international export market is the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[266]](#cite_note-271)[[267]](#cite_note-272) [Scotch whisky](/wiki/Scotch_whisky "Scotch whisky") is one of Scotland's more known goods of economic activity.[[268]](#cite_note-Facts_&_Figures-273) In 2024, whisky exports from Scotland contributed £5.4 billion in revenue towards the economy, accounting for 74% of all Scottish food and drink exports internationally in 2023.[[268]](#cite_note-Facts_&_Figures-273) The Scotch whisky industry supports over 41,000 jobs in the sector across Scotland.[[268]](#cite_note-Facts_&_Figures-273) Tourism is regarded as a major component within the Scottish economy,[[269]](#cite_note-274) supporting over 245,000 related jobs across the sector and contributing £10.8 billion in revenue in 2023, 6% of the overall Scottish economy.[[270]](#cite_note-275) The most visited tourist attractions in the country include [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle"), the [National Museum of Scotland](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Scotland "National Museum of Scotland"), the [Battle of Culloden Visitor Centre](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden"), [Edinburgh Zoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Zoo "Edinburgh Zoo"), [Stirling Castle](/wiki/Stirling_Castle "Stirling Castle") and [Urquhart Castle](/wiki/Urquhart_Castle "Urquhart Castle").[[271]](#cite_note-276) A considerable number of [Scottish companies](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Scotland "List of companies of Scotland") have established international reputations and trade worldwide in a variety of sectors, including [Rockstar North](/wiki/Rockstar_North "Rockstar North") (gaming), [Pringle of Scotland](/wiki/Pringle_of_Scotland "Pringle of Scotland") and [Lyle & Scott](/wiki/Lyle_%26_Scott "Lyle & Scott") (fashion and textiles), [A.G. Barr](/wiki/A.G._Barr "A.G. Barr") (soft drinks manufacturing), [Alexander Dennis](/wiki/Alexander_Dennis "Alexander Dennis") (bus manufacturing), [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group") (transportation), [Tunnock's](/wiki/Tunnock%27s "Tunnock's") (confectionery) and [Johnnie Walker](/wiki/Johnnie_Walker "Johnnie Walker") (whisky).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg/250px-North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg)

[Oil extraction in Scottish waters](/wiki/Oil_industry_in_Scotland "Oil industry in Scotland") in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea")

Scotland was one of the industrial powerhouses of Europe from the time of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") onwards, being a world leader in manufacturing.[[272]](#cite_note-277) This left a legacy in the diversity of goods and services which Scotland produces, from textiles, [whisky](/wiki/Whisky "Whisky") and [shortbread](/wiki/Shortbread "Shortbread") to jet engines, buses, computer software, [investment management](/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management") and other related financial services.[[273]](#cite_note-278) In common with most other advanced industrialised economies, Scotland has seen a decline in the importance of both manufacturing industries and primary-based extractive industries. This has been combined with a rise in the [service](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") sector of the economy, which has grown to be the largest sector in Scotland.[[274]](#cite_note-279) Historically, the Scottish economy was dominated by [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") underpinned by shipbuilding in Glasgow, coal mining and [steel industries](/wiki/Steel_making "Steel making"). Petroleum-related industries associated with the extraction of [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") have also been important employers since the 1970s, especially in the north-east of Scotland. De-industrialisation during the 1970s and 1980s saw a shift from a manufacturing focus towards a more [service](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_economic_activity "Tertiary sector of economic activity")-oriented economy. As a result, the countries primary economic sectors include [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Scotland "Agriculture in Scotland"), [forestry](/wiki/Forestry_in_Scotland "Forestry in Scotland"), [fisheries](/wiki/Fishing_in_Scotland "Fishing in Scotland"), manufacturing and construction, [oil and gas extraction](/wiki/Oil_industry_in_Scotland "Oil industry in Scotland"), science, technology, financial services, [food and drink](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Scotland "Cuisine of Scotland") and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Scotland "Tourism in Scotland"), with the [space industry](/wiki/Space_industry_of_Scotland "Space industry of Scotland"), [renewable energy](/wiki/Energy_in_Scotland "Energy in Scotland") and [financial technologies](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") sectors being considered as developing industries across the country.[[275]](#cite_note-280) Scotland's fisheries sector and associated zone is the fourth largest amongst European nations.[[276]](#cite_note-281)

The [Scottish National Investment Bank](/wiki/Scottish_National_Investment_Bank "Scottish National Investment Bank") was established in 2020 by the Scottish Government, which uses public money to fund commercial projects across Scotland in the hope that this seed capital will encourage further private investment, to help develop a fairer, more sustainable economy. £2 billion of taxpayers' money was earmarked for the bank.[[277]](#cite_note-bbc-282)

### Income and poverty

Main article: [Income tax in Scotland](/wiki/Income_tax_in_Scotland "Income tax in Scotland")

Further information: [Scottish variable rate](/wiki/Scottish_variable_rate "Scottish variable rate"), [Scottish Consolidated Fund](/wiki/Scottish_Consolidated_Fund "Scottish Consolidated Fund"), and [Government Expenditure and Revenue Scotland](/wiki/Government_Expenditure_and_Revenue_Scotland "Government Expenditure and Revenue Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edinburgh_%2841404375864%29.jpg/250px-Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edinburgh_%2841404375864%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_on_the_mound,_Edinburgh_(41404375864).jpg)

The [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland") is one of the oldest banks in the world, and the first bank in Europe to successfully print its own [banknotes](/wiki/Banknotes "Banknotes")

The average weekly income for workplace-based employees in Scotland is £573,[[278]](#cite_note-obs-283) and £576 for home-based employees.[[278]](#cite_note-obs-283) Scotland has the third highest median gross salary in the United Kingdom at £26,007, which is higher than the overall UK average annual salary of £25,971.[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284) With an average of £14.28, Scotland has the third highest median hourly rate (excluding overtime working hours) of the countries of the United Kingdom, and like the annual salary, this is higher than the average UK figure as a whole.[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284) The highest paid industries in Scotland tend of be in the [utility](/wiki/Utility "Utility") [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity"), [gas](/wiki/Gas "Gas") and [air conditioning](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning") sectors,[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284) while industries like [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), accommodation and food and drink tend to be the lowest paid.[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284) The top [local authority](/wiki/Local_authority "Local authority") for pay, based on place of residence, is [East Renfrewshire](/wiki/East_Renfrewshire "East Renfrewshire") (£20.87 per hour).[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284)

The top local authority for pay based on place of work is [East Ayrshire](/wiki/East_Ayrshire "East Ayrshire") (£16.92 per hour). Scotland's cities commonly have the largest salaries in Scotland based on place of work.[[279]](#cite_note-dig-284) 2021/2022 data indicate that there were 2.6 million [dwellings](/wiki/Dwelling "Dwelling") across Scotland, with 318,369 [local authority](/wiki/Local_authority "Local authority") dwellings.[[280]](#cite_note-285) A typical price for a house in Scotland was £195,000 in August 2022.[[281]](#cite_note-286)

Between 2016 and 2020, the Scottish Government estimated that 10% of people in Scotland were in persistent poverty following housing costs,[[282]](#cite_note-287) with similar rates of persistent poverty for children (10%), working-age adults (10%) and pensioners (11%).[[283]](#cite_note-persistentpoverty2022-288) Persistent child poverty rates had seen a relatively sharp drop; however, the accuracy of this was deemed to be questionable due to various factors such as households re-entering the longitudinal sample allowing data gaps to be filled.[[283]](#cite_note-persistentpoverty2022-288) The Scottish Government introduced the Scottish Child Payment in 2021 for low-income families with children under six years of age in an attempt to reduce child poverty rates, with families receiving a payment of roughly £1,040 per year.[[284]](#cite_note-289) As of October 2023, 10% of the Scottish population were estimated to be living in poverty.[[285]](#cite_note-290)

### Currency

Main article: [Banknotes of Scotland](/wiki/Banknotes_of_Scotland "Banknotes of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e0/RoyBankScotland100.jpg/250px-RoyBankScotland100.jpg)](/wiki/File:RoyBankScotland100.jpg)

Example of a [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") banknote

Although the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") is the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") for the UK, three Scottish [clearing banks](/wiki/Clearing_bank_(United_Kingdom) "Clearing bank (United Kingdom)") issue [Sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") [banknotes](/wiki/Banknote "Banknote"): the [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland"), the [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") and the [Clydesdale Bank](/wiki/Clydesdale_Bank "Clydesdale Bank"). The Bank of Scotland was the first bank in Europe to successfully print its own paper currency, with the new banknotes entering circulation in 1696, making the Bank of Scotland the longest continuous issuer of banknotes in the world.[[286]](#cite_note-291) The issuing of banknotes by [retail banks](/wiki/Retail_bank "Retail bank") in Scotland is subject to the [Banking Act 2009](/wiki/Banking_Act_2009 "Banking Act 2009"), which repealed all earlier legislation under which banknote issuance was regulated, and the Scottish and Northern Ireland Banknote Regulations 2009.[[287]](#cite_note-292)

The value of the Scottish banknotes in circulation in 2013 was £3.8 billion, underwritten by the Bank of England using funds deposited by each clearing bank, under the [Banking Act 2009](/wiki/Banking_Act_2009 "Banking Act 2009"), to cover the total value of such notes in circulation.[[288]](#cite_note-Banknotes-293)

### Infrastructure and transportation

Main article: [Transport in Scotland](/wiki/Transport_in_Scotland "Transport in Scotland")

Further information: [List of airports in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Scotland "List of airports in Scotland") and [List of ports and harbours in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_ports_and_harbours_in_Scotland "List of ports and harbours in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Airport_and_plane_Barra_airstrip_-_geograph.org.uk_-_852094.jpg/250px-Airport_and_plane_Barra_airstrip_-_geograph.org.uk_-_852094.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airport_and_plane_Barra_airstrip_-_geograph.org.uk_-_852094.jpg)

[Barra Airport](/wiki/Barra_Airport "Barra Airport"), the only airport in the world to use a [tidal](/wiki/Tide "Tide") [beach](/wiki/Beach "Beach") as the runway

Scotland has [five international airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom_and_the_British_Crown_Dependencies#Scotland "List of airports in the United Kingdom and the British Crown Dependencies") operating scheduled services to Europe, North America and Asia, as well as domestic services to England, Northern Ireland and Wales and within Scotland.[[289]](#cite_note-294) [Highlands and Islands Airports](/wiki/Highlands_and_Islands_Airports "Highlands and Islands Airports") operates eleven airports across the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"), [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney"), [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") and the [Western Isles](/wiki/Western_Isles "Western Isles"), which are primarily used for short distance, public service operations, although Inverness Airport has a number of scheduled flights to destinations across the UK and mainland Europe.[[290]](#cite_note-295) Edinburgh Airport is currently Scotland's busiest airport, handling over 13 million passengers in 2017.[[291]](#cite_note-296) It is also the UK's 6th busiest airport. The airline [Loganair](/wiki/Loganair "Loganair") has its headquarters at Glasgow Airport and markets itself as *Scotland's Airline*.[[292]](#cite_note-297)

[Network Rail](/wiki/Network_Rail "Network Rail") owns and operates the fixed infrastructure assets of the railway system in Scotland, while the Scottish Government retains overall responsibility for rail strategy and funding in Scotland.[[293]](#cite_note-Office_of_Rail_Regulation-298) Scotland's rail network has 359 railway stations and around 1,710 miles (2,760 km) of track.[[294]](#cite_note-299) In 2022–23 there were 64 million passenger journeys on [ScotRail](/wiki/ScotRail "ScotRail") rail services.[[295]](#cite_note-300) On 1 January 2006, [Transport Scotland](/wiki/Transport_Scotland "Transport Scotland") was established, which would oversee the regulation of railways in Scotland and administer major rail projects.[[296]](#cite_note-TransportSco-301)
Since April 2022, Transport Scotland has taken ScotRail back into public ownership via its [operator of last resort](/wiki/Operator_of_last_resort "Operator of last resort"), [Scottish Rail Holdings](/wiki/Scottish_Rail_Holdings "Scottish Rail Holdings").[[297]](#cite_note-302) It did the same with the Caledonian Sleeper service in June 2023.[[298]](#cite_note-ts323-303)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/ScotRail_Class_170_Forth_Bridge.jpg/250px-ScotRail_Class_170_Forth_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:ScotRail_Class_170_Forth_Bridge.jpg)

The [Forth Bridge](/wiki/Forth_Bridge "Forth Bridge"), a well-known structure in Scottish rail and a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")

The [Glasgow Subway](/wiki/Glasgow_Subway "Glasgow Subway") is the only [underground system](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") in Scotland. It opened on 14 December 1896, making it the third-oldest underground network in the world after the [Budapest Metro](/wiki/Budapest_Metro "Budapest Metro") and the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"). It is owned and operated by [Strathclyde Partnership for Transport](/wiki/Strathclyde_Partnership_for_Transport "Strathclyde Partnership for Transport").[[299]](#cite_note-304)

The Scottish motorways and major [trunk roads](/wiki/Trunk_roads "Trunk roads") are managed by Transport Scotland. The remainder of the road network is managed by the [Scottish local authorities](/wiki/Scottish_local_authorities "Scottish local authorities") in each of their areas.

Regular ferry services operate between the Scottish mainland and outlying islands. Ferries serving both the [Inner](/wiki/Inner_Hebrides "Inner Hebrides") and [Outer Hebrides](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides") are principally operated by the [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") [Caledonian MacBrayne](/wiki/Caledonian_MacBrayne "Caledonian MacBrayne").[[300]](#cite_note-305)[[301]](#cite_note-306) Services to the [Northern Isles](/wiki/Northern_Isles "Northern Isles") are operated by [Serco](/wiki/Serco "Serco"). Other routes, such as southwest Scotland to Northern Ireland, are served by multiple companies.[[302]](#cite_note-307) [DFDS Seaways](/wiki/DFDS_Seaways "DFDS Seaways") operated a freight-only [Rosyth – Zeebrugge ferry service](/wiki/Rosyth_%E2%80%93_Zeebrugge_ferry_service "Rosyth – Zeebrugge ferry service"), until a fire damaged the vessel DFDS were using.[[303]](#cite_note-308) A passenger service was also operated between 2002 and 2010.[[304]](#cite_note-309)

### Science, technology and energy

Further information: [Nuclear power in Scotland](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Scotland "Nuclear power in Scotland"), [Renewable energy in Scotland](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Scotland "Renewable energy in Scotland"), [Oil industry in Scotland](/wiki/Oil_industry_in_Scotland "Oil industry in Scotland"), [Energy in Scotland](/wiki/Energy_in_Scotland "Energy in Scotland"), and [Space industry of Scotland](/wiki/Space_industry_of_Scotland "Space industry of Scotland")

[![A donkey](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Synthetic_Production_of_Penicillin_TR1468_crop.jpg/250px-Synthetic_Production_of_Penicillin_TR1468_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Synthetic_Production_of_Penicillin_TR1468_crop.jpg)

Sir [Alexander Fleming](/wiki/Alexander_Fleming "Alexander Fleming"), the discoverer of the world's first broadly effective [antibiotic](/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic") substance – [penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin")

[![A trout](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/John_Logie_Baird_in_1917.jpg/250px-John_Logie_Baird_in_1917.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Logie_Baird_in_1917.jpg)

Scottish inventor [John Logie Baird](/wiki/John_Logie_Baird "John Logie Baird") demonstrated the first working television system on 26 January 1926.[[305]](#cite_note-310)

Scotland's primary sources of energy are provided through renewable energy (61.8%), nuclear (25.7%) and fossil fuel generation (10.9%).[[306]](#cite_note-:10-311) [Whitelee Wind Farm](/wiki/Whitelee_Wind_Farm "Whitelee Wind Farm") is the largest onshore [wind farm](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") in the United Kingdom, and was Europe's largest onshore wind farm for some time.[[307]](#cite_note-312) [Tidal power](/wiki/Tidal_power "Tidal power") is an emerging source of energy in Scotland. The [MeyGen](/wiki/MeyGen "MeyGen") tidal stream energy plant in the north of the country is claimed to be the largest tidal stream energy project in the world.[[308]](#cite_note-313) In Scotland, 98.6% of all electricity used was from renewable sources. This is minus net exports.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-311) Between October 2021 and September 2022 63.1% of all electricity generated in Scotland was from renewable sources, 83.6% was classed as low carbon and 14.5% was from fossil fuels.[[309]](#cite_note-314) The Scottish Government has a target to have the equivalent of 50% of the energy for Scotland's heat, transport and electricity consumption to be supplied from renewable sources by 2030.[[310]](#cite_note-315) They have stated that, in 2022, the equivalent of 113% of the country's overall electrical consumption was produced by renewable energy, making it the highest recorded figure of renewable energy generated to date.[[311]](#cite_note-316)

[Scotland's inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Scottish inventions and discoveries") are said to have revolutionised human technology and have played a major role in the creation of the modern world. Such inventions – the [television](/wiki/Television "Television"), the [telephone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone"), [refrigerators](/wiki/Refrigerators "Refrigerators"), the [MRI scanner](/wiki/MRI_scanner "MRI scanner"), [flushing toilets](/wiki/Flushing_toilet "Flushing toilet") and the [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine") – are said to have been possible by Scotland's universities and parish schools, together with the commitment Scots had to education during the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment").[[312]](#cite_note-317) [Alexander Fleming](/wiki/Alexander_Fleming "Alexander Fleming") is responsible for the discovery of the world's first broadly effective [antibiotic](/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic") substance, which he named [penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin"), earning him a [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") in 1945.[[313]](#cite_note-lesprixnobel-318)[[314]](#cite_note-319)[[315]](#cite_note-320) Modern Scottish inventions – the [Falkirk Wheel](/wiki/Falkirk_Wheel "Falkirk Wheel") and the [Glasgow Tower](/wiki/Glasgow_Tower "Glasgow Tower") – hold world records for being the only rotating [boat lift](/wiki/Boat_lift "Boat lift") and the tallest fully rotating freestanding structure in the world respectively.[[316]](#cite_note-321)[[317]](#cite_note-322)

Scotland's [space industry](/wiki/Space_industry_of_Scotland "Space industry of Scotland") is a world leader in sustainable space technology,[[318]](#cite_note-auto-323)[[319]](#cite_note-324) and, according to the [UK Space Agency](/wiki/UK_Space_Agency "UK Space Agency"), there are 173 space companies currently operating in Scotland as of May 2021.[[320]](#cite_note-325) These include spacecraft manufacturers, launch providers, downstream data analyzers, and research organisations.[[321]](#cite_note-326) The space industry in Scotland is projected to generate £2billion in income for Scotland's space cluster by 2030.[[318]](#cite_note-auto-323) Scottish space industry jobs represent almost one in five of all UK space industry employment.[[322]](#cite_note-327) In addition to its space industry, Scotland is home to two planned [spaceports](/wiki/Spaceports "Spaceports") – [Sutherland spaceport](/wiki/Sutherland_spaceport "Sutherland spaceport") and [SaxaVord Spaceport](/wiki/SaxaVord_Spaceport "SaxaVord Spaceport").[[323]](#cite_note-328)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Scotland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Scotland "Demographics of Scotland")

See also: [Languages of Scotland](/wiki/Languages_of_Scotland "Languages of Scotland"), [Religion in Scotland](/wiki/Religion_in_Scotland "Religion in Scotland"), and [Scottish people](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people")

### Population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Scotland_population_cartogram.svg/250px-Scotland_population_cartogram.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Scotland_population_cartogram.svg)

Scotland population [cartogram](/wiki/Cartogram "Cartogram"). The size of councils is in proportion to their population.

During the 1820s, many Scots migrated from Scotland to countries such as [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), principally from the Highlands which remained poor in comparison to elsewhere in Scotland.[[324]](#cite_note-329) The Highlands was the only part of mainland Britain with a recurrent famine.[[325]](#cite_note-330) A small range of products were exported from the region, which had negligible industrial production and a continued population growth that tested the subsistence agriculture. These problems, and the desire to improve agriculture and profits were the driving forces of the ongoing [Highland Clearances](/wiki/Highland_Clearances "Highland Clearances"), in which many of the population of the Highlands suffered eviction as lands were enclosed, principally so that they could be used for sheep farming. The first phase of the clearances followed patterns of agricultural change throughout Britain. The second phase was driven by overpopulation, the [Highland Potato Famine](/wiki/Highland_Potato_Famine "Highland Potato Famine") and the collapse of industries that had relied on the wartime economy of the Napoleonic Wars.[[326]](#cite_note-331)

The population of Scotland grew steadily in the 19th century, from 1,608,000 in the census of 1801 to 2,889,000 in 1851 and 4,472,000 in 1901.[[327]](#cite_note-332) Even with the development of industry, there were not enough good jobs. As a result, during the period 1841–1931, about 2 million Scots migrated to North America and Australia, and another 750,000 Scots relocated to England.[[328]](#cite_note-Huston&Knox2001pxxxii-333) Caused by the advent of [refrigeration](/wiki/Refrigeration "Refrigeration") and imports of lamb, mutton and wool from overseas, the 1870s brought with them a collapse of sheep prices and an abrupt halt in the previous sheep farming boom.[[329]](#cite_note-:0-334)

Scotland is the [most sparsely populated](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") of the United Kingdom.[[330]](#cite_note-335)[[331]](#cite_note-336) In August 2012, the Scottish population reached an all-time high of 5.25 million people.[[332]](#cite_note-thecourier1-337) The reasons given were that, in Scotland, births were outnumbering the number of deaths, and immigrants were moving to Scotland from overseas. In 2011, 43,700 people moved from Wales, Northern Ireland or England to live in Scotland.[[332]](#cite_note-thecourier1-337) The [most recent census in Scotland](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census#2022_Census_for_Scotland "2021 United Kingdom census") was conducted by the Scottish Government and the [National Records of Scotland](/wiki/National_Records_of_Scotland "National Records of Scotland") in March 2022.[[333]](#cite_note-auto2-338) The population of Scotland at the 2022 Census was 5,436,600, the highest ever,[[333]](#cite_note-auto2-338) beating the previous record of 5,295,400 at the 2011 Census. It was 5,062,011 at the 2001 Census.[[334]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-339) An ONS estimate for 2024 was 5,546,900.[[5]](#cite_note-ONS_mid-year_pop_est-7) In the 2011 Census, 62% of Scotland's population stated their [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity") as '[Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") only', 18% as 'Scottish and British', 8% as 'British only', and 4% chose 'other identity only'.[[335]](#cite_note-340)

Throughout its history, Scotland has long had a tradition of migration from Scotland and immigration into Scotland. In 2021, the Scottish Government released figures showing that an estimated 41,000 people had immigrated from other international countries into Scotland, while an average of 22,100 people had migrated from Scotland. Scottish Government data from 2002 shows that by 2021, there had been a sharp increase in immigration to Scotland, with 2002 estimates standing at 27,800 immigrants. While immigration had increased from 2002, migration from Scotland had dropped, with 2002 estimates standing at 26,200 people migrating from Scotland.[[336]](#cite_note-341)

### Urbanisation

See also: [Cities of Scotland](/wiki/Cities_of_Scotland "Cities of Scotland") and [List of towns and cities in Scotland by population](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Scotland_by_population "List of towns and cities in Scotland by population")

Although [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") is the capital of Scotland, the largest city is Glasgow, which has just over 584,000 inhabitants. The [Greater Glasgow](/wiki/Greater_Glasgow "Greater Glasgow") conurbation, with a population of almost 1.2 million, is home to nearly a quarter of Scotland's population.[[337]](#cite_note-342) The [Central Belt](/wiki/Central_Belt "Central Belt") is where most of the main towns and cities of Scotland are located, including Glasgow, Edinburgh, Dundee, and Perth. Scotland's only major city outside the Central Belt is Aberdeen. The [Scottish Lowlands](/wiki/Scottish_Lowlands "Scottish Lowlands") host 80% of the total population, where the [Central Belt](/wiki/Central_Belt "Central Belt") accounts for 3.5 million people.

In general, only the more accessible and larger islands remain inhabited. Currently, fewer than 90 remain inhabited. The Southern Uplands is essentially rural and dominated by agriculture and forestry.[[338]](#cite_note-343)[[339]](#cite_note-344) Because of housing problems in Glasgow and Edinburgh, [five new towns](/wiki/New_towns_in_the_United_Kingdom#Scotland "New towns in the United Kingdom") were designated between 1947 and 1966. They are [East Kilbride](/wiki/East_Kilbride "East Kilbride"), [Glenrothes](/wiki/Glenrothes "Glenrothes"), [Cumbernauld](/wiki/Cumbernauld "Cumbernauld"), [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston,_West_Lothian "Livingston, West Lothian"), and [Irvine](/wiki/Irvine,_North_Ayrshire "Irvine, North Ayrshire").[[340]](#cite_note-345)

The largest council area by population is [Glasgow City](/wiki/Glasgow_City_Council "Glasgow City Council"), with [Highland](/wiki/Highland_(council_area) "Highland (council area)") being the largest in terms of geographical area.
