### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Scotland](/wiki/Languages_of_Scotland "Languages of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Welcome_to_Scotland_sign_on_the_A1_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4057371.jpg/250px-Welcome_to_Scotland_sign_on_the_A1_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4057371.jpg)](/wiki/File:Welcome_to_Scotland_sign_on_the_A1_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4057371.jpg)

The [Anglo-Scottish border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border"), welcoming travellers in both English and [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic")

Scotland has three [indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language"): English, [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), and [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic").[[342]](#cite_note-347)[[343]](#cite_note-348) Scottish Standard English, a variety of English as spoken in Scotland, is at one end of a bipolar [linguistic continuum](/wiki/Linguistic_continuum "Linguistic continuum"), with broad [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") at the other.[[344]](#cite_note-349) Scottish Standard English may have been influenced to varying degrees by Scots.[[345]](#cite_note-Stuart-Smith_J._2008._p.48-350)[[346]](#cite_note-351) [Highland English](/wiki/Highland_English "Highland English") is spoken in that region, while Gaelic is mostly spoken in the [Western Isles](/wiki/Western_Isles "Western Isles"), where it continues to be used by a large proportion of residents.

Overall, the use of Scotland's indigenous languages other than English has declined since the 19th century. The 2011 census indicated that 63% of the population had "no skills in Scots".[[341]](#cite_note-2022_census-346) The use of Gaelic is confined to 1% of the population.[[347]](#cite_note-352) The number of Gaelic speakers in Scotland dropped from 250,000 in 1881 to 60,000 in 2008.[[348]](#cite_note-353) Across the whole of Scotland, the 2011 census showed that 25,000 people (0.49% of the population) used Gaelic at home. The most common language spoken at home in Scotland after English and Scots is Polish, with about 1.1% of the population, or 54,000 people.[[349]](#cite_note-354)[[350]](#cite_note-355)

Immigration since World War II has given Glasgow, Edinburgh, and Dundee small South Asian communities.[[351]](#cite_note-356) In 2011, there were an estimated 49,000 ethnically Pakistani people living in Scotland, making them the largest non-White ethnic group.[[352]](#cite_note-ethnicity-357) The 2004 [enlargement of the European Union](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union") spurred an increase in migration from [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") to Scotland, and the 2011 census indicated that 61,000 [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") lived there.[[352]](#cite_note-ethnicity-357)[[353]](#cite_note-358)

There are many more people with Scottish ancestry living abroad than the total population of Scotland. In the 2000 Census, 9.2 million Americans self-reported some degree of [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_American "Scottish American") descent.[[354]](#cite_note-359) [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster")'s Protestant population is mainly of Lowland Scottish descent,[[355]](#cite_note-360) and it is estimated that there are more than 27 million descendants of the [Scots-Irish](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_people "Ulster Scots people") migration now living in the US.[[356]](#cite_note-361)[[357]](#cite_note-362) In Canada, the [Scottish-Canadian](/wiki/Scottish-Canadian "Scottish-Canadian") community accounts for 4.7 million people.[[358]](#cite_note-cancensus-363) About 20% of the original European settler population of New Zealand came from Scotland.[[359]](#cite_note-364)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Scotland](/wiki/Religion_in_Scotland "Religion in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/St.Giles_Cathedral_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-St.Giles_Cathedral_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:St.Giles_Cathedral_-_panoramio.jpg)

[High Kirk of Edinburgh](/wiki/High_Kirk_of_Edinburgh "High Kirk of Edinburgh")

As per the 2022 Census, a majority of Scots (51.12%) reported not following any religion. The most practiced religion is Christianity (38.79%), mostly the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") (20.36%) and [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Scotland "Catholic Church in Scotland") (13.3%).[[360]](#cite_note-:1-365) In almost every council area, the most common response to the census question was "No religion," except in [Na h-Eileanan Siar](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides") and [Inverclyde](/wiki/Inverclyde "Inverclyde"), where the Church of Scotland (35.3%) and Catholicism (33.4%) were the most common responses, respectively.[[360]](#cite_note-:1-365)

Forms of [Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity_in_Scotland "History of Christianity in Scotland") have dominated religious life in what is now Scotland for more than 1,400 years.[[361]](#cite_note-366)[[362]](#cite_note-367)
Since the [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation") of 1560, the [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church") (the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), also known as [The Kirk](/wiki/Kirk_(word) "Kirk (word)")) has been [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") in orientation and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_theology "Reformed theology") in theology. Since 1689 it has had a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") system of church government independent from the state.[[15]](#cite_note-Keay-20) Its membership dropped just below 300,000 in 2020 (5% of the total population)[[363]](#cite_note-368)[[364]](#cite_note-scotsman.com-369)[[365]](#cite_note-370) The Church operates a territorial parish structure, with every community in Scotland having a local congregation.

Scotland also has a significant [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church_in_Scotland "Roman Catholic Church in Scotland") population with 13.3% professing that faith, particularly in Greater Glasgow and the north-west.[[366]](#cite_note-371)[[360]](#cite_note-:1-365) After the Reformation, Roman Catholicism in Scotland continued in the Highlands and some western islands like [Uist](/wiki/Uist "Uist") and [Barra](/wiki/Barra "Barra"), and it was strengthened during the 19th century by immigration from Ireland. Other Christian denominations in Scotland include the [Free Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Church_of_Scotland_(since_1900) "Free Church of Scotland (since 1900)"), and various other Presbyterian offshoots. Scotland's third largest church is the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church").[[367]](#cite_note-372)

Other minority faiths include [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Scotland "Islam in Scotland") (2.2%), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Scotland "Hinduism in Scotland") (0.55%), Sikhism and Buddhism.[[360]](#cite_note-:1-365)[[368]](#cite_note-2011_census_religion-373)[[369]](#cite_note-GROSCOT-374) The [Samyé Ling](/wiki/Samy%C3%A9_Ling "Samyé Ling") monastery near [Eskdalemuir](/wiki/Eskdalemuir "Eskdalemuir"), which celebrated its 40th anniversary in 2007, is the oldest [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") monastery in Western Europe.[[370]](#cite_note-375)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Scotland](/wiki/Education_in_Scotland "Education in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/%2B_908_wurde_St_Andrews_bereits_Bischohfssitz._13_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-%2B_908_wurde_St_Andrews_bereits_Bischohfssitz._13_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%2B_908_wurde_St_Andrews_bereits_Bischohfssitz._13_(cropped).jpg)

Founded in 1413, the [University of St. Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St._Andrews "University of St. Andrews") is the oldest in Scotland and one of the oldest worldwide[[371]](#cite_note-376)

The [Scottish education system](/wiki/Education_in_Scotland "Education in Scotland") has always had a characteristic emphasis on a [broad education](/wiki/Liberal_education "Liberal education").[[372]](#cite_note-377) In the 15th century, the Humanist emphasis on education cumulated with the passing of the [Education Act 1496](/wiki/Education_Act_1496 "Education Act 1496"), which decreed that all sons of barons and freeholders of substance should attend grammar schools to learn "perfyct Latyne", resulting in an increase in literacy among a male and wealthy elite.[[373]](#cite_note-Bawcutt&Williams2006pp29-30-378) In the Reformation, the 1560 *[First Book of Discipline](/wiki/First_Book_of_Discipline "First Book of Discipline")* set out a plan for a school in every parish, but this proved financially impossible.[[374]](#cite_note-379) In 1616 an [act in Privy council](/wiki/School_Establishment_Act_1616 "School Establishment Act 1616") commanded every parish to establish a school.[[375]](#cite_note-380) By the late seventeenth century there was a largely complete network of parish schools in the lowlands, but in the Highlands basic education was still lacking in many areas.[[376]](#cite_note-Anderson2003-381) Education remained a matter for the church rather than the state until the [Education (Scotland) Act 1872](/wiki/Education_(Scotland)_Act_1872 "Education (Scotland) Act 1872").[[377]](#cite_note-382)

Education in Scotland is the responsibility of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") and is overseen by its executive agency [Education Scotland](/wiki/Education_Scotland "Education Scotland").[[378]](#cite_note-383) The *[Curriculum for Excellence](/wiki/Curriculum_for_Excellence "Curriculum for Excellence")*, Scotland's national school curriculum, presently provides the curricular framework for children and young people from age 3 to 18.[[379]](#cite_note-384) All 3- and 4-year-old children in Scotland are entitled to a free nursery place. Formal primary education begins at approximately 5 years old and lasts for 7 years (P1–P7); children in Scotland study National Qualifications of the [Curriculum for Excellence](/wiki/Curriculum_for_Excellence "Curriculum for Excellence") between the ages of 14 and 18. The school leaving age is 16, after which students may choose to remain at school and study further qualifications. A small number of students at certain [private schools](/wiki/List_of_private_schools_in_Scotland "List of private schools in Scotland") may follow the [English system](/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England") and study towards [GCSEs](/wiki/GCSE "GCSE") and [A](/wiki/Advanced_Level_(UK) "Advanced Level (UK)") and [AS-Levels](/wiki/Advanced_Level_(UK) "Advanced Level (UK)") instead.[[380]](#cite_note-385)

There are fifteen [Scottish universities](/wiki/Scottish_universities "Scottish universities"), some of which are among the [oldest in the world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation").[[381]](#cite_note-386)[[382]](#cite_note-387) The four universities founded before the end of the 16th century – the [University of St Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews "University of St Andrews"), the [University of Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow"), the [University of Aberdeen](/wiki/University_of_Aberdeen "University of Aberdeen") and the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") – are collectively known as the [ancient universities of Scotland](/wiki/Ancient_universities_of_Scotland "Ancient universities of Scotland"), all of which rank among the 200 best universities in the world in the [THE rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings"), with Edinburgh placing in the top 50.[[383]](#cite_note-388) Scotland had more universities per capita in [QS' World University Rankings'](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") top 100 in 2012 than any other nation.[[384]](#cite_note-389) The country produces 1% of the world's [published research](/wiki/Academic_publishing "Academic publishing") with less than 0.1% of the world's population, and higher education institutions account for 9% of Scotland's service sector exports.[[385]](#cite_note-390)[[386]](#cite_note-391) Scotland's University Courts are the only bodies in Scotland authorised to award degrees.

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Scotland](/wiki/Health_in_Scotland "Health in Scotland") and [Healthcare in Scotland](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Scotland "Healthcare in Scotland")

Further information: [List of hospitals in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Scotland "List of hospitals in Scotland") and [Social care in Scotland](/wiki/Social_care_in_Scotland "Social care in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/View_along_the_access_road_towards_the_new_Southern_General_Hospital_%28geograph_4542089%29.jpg/250px-View_along_the_access_road_towards_the_new_Southern_General_Hospital_%28geograph_4542089%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_along_the_access_road_towards_the_new_Southern_General_Hospital_(geograph_4542089).jpg)

[NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland")'s [Queen Elizabeth University Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_University_Hospital "Queen Elizabeth University Hospital"), Glasgow. It is the largest hospital campus in Europe.[[387]](#cite_note-392)

Health care in Scotland is mainly provided by [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland"), Scotland's public health care system. This was founded by the [National Health Service (Scotland) Act 1947](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(Scotland)_Act_1947 "National Health Service (Scotland) Act 1947") (later repealed by the National Health Service (Scotland) Act 1978) that took effect on 5 July 1948 to coincide with the launch of the NHS in England and Wales. Prior to 1948, half of Scotland's landmass was already covered by state-funded health care, provided by the [Highlands and Islands Medical Service](/wiki/Highlands_and_Islands_Medical_Service "Highlands and Islands Medical Service").[[388]](#cite_note-393) Healthcare policy and funding is the responsibility of the Scottish Government's [Health Directorates](/wiki/Scottish_Government_Health_Directorates "Scottish Government Health Directorates"). In 2014, the NHS in Scotland had around 140,000 staff.[[389]](#cite_note-394)

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in Scotland is below the replacement rate of 2.1 (the TFR was 1.73 in 2011[[390]](#cite_note-395)). The majority of births are to unmarried women (51.3% of births were outside of marriage in 2012[[391]](#cite_note-396)).

Life expectancy for those born in Scotland between 2012 and 2014 is 77.1 years for males and 81.1 years for females.[[392]](#cite_note-life_expectancy_2015-397) This is the lowest of any of the four countries of the UK.[[392]](#cite_note-life_expectancy_2015-397) The number of hospital admissions in Scotland for diseases such as cancer was 2,528 in 2002. Over the next ten years, by 2012, this had increased to 2,669.[[393]](#cite_note-hospitaladmissions-398) Hospital admissions for other diseases, such as coronary heart disease (CHD) were lower, with 727 admissions in 2002, and decreasing to 489 in 2012.[[393]](#cite_note-hospitaladmissions-398)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Scotland](/wiki/Culture_of_Scotland "Culture of Scotland")

### World Heritage Sites

Main article: [List of World Heritage Sites in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Scotland "List of World Heritage Sites in Scotland")

Scotland has seven [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Scotland "List of World Heritage Sites in Scotland") – [St. Kilda](/wiki/St_Kilda,_Scotland "St Kilda, Scotland"), [Edinburgh Old Town](/wiki/Edinburgh_Old_Town "Edinburgh Old Town") and [New Town](/wiki/Edinburgh_New_Town "Edinburgh New Town"), The [Heart of Neolithic Orkney](/wiki/Heart_of_Neolithic_Orkney "Heart of Neolithic Orkney"), [New Lanark](/wiki/New_Lanark "New Lanark"), The [Antonine Wall](/wiki/Antonine_Wall "Antonine Wall"), The [Forth Bridge](/wiki/Forth_Bridge "Forth Bridge") and The [Flow Country](/wiki/Flow_Country "Flow Country").[[394]](#cite_note-399) [Zenith of Iron Age Shetland](/wiki/Zenith_of_Iron_Age_Shetland "Zenith of Iron Age Shetland") is currently a candidate for World Heritage Site status from [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[395]](#cite_note-400) [Historic Environment Scotland](/wiki/Historic_Environment_Scotland "Historic Environment Scotland") is responsible for 'cultural' sites as part of their wider responsibility towards the historic environment. The [Environment Directorate](/wiki/Environment_Directorate "Environment Directorate") is responsible for natural sites.[[396]](#cite_note-401)

### Celtic connections

Further information: [Celtic nations](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations")

As one of the [Celtic nations](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations"), Scotland and Scottish culture are represented at inter-Celtic events at home and over the world. Scotland hosts several music festivals including [Celtic Connections](/wiki/Celtic_Connections "Celtic Connections") (Glasgow), and the [Hebridean Celtic Festival](/wiki/Hebridean_Celtic_Festival "Hebridean Celtic Festival") (Stornoway). Festivals celebrating Celtic culture, such as [Festival Interceltique de Lorient](/wiki/Festival_Interceltique_de_Lorient "Festival Interceltique de Lorient") ([Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany")), the [Pan Celtic Festival](/wiki/Pan_Celtic_Festival "Pan Celtic Festival") (Ireland), and the National Celtic Festival ([Portarlington](/wiki/Portarlington,_Victoria "Portarlington, Victoria"), Australia), feature elements of Scottish culture such as language, music and dance.[[397]](#cite_note-Celtic_connections_1-402)[[398]](#cite_note-Festival_1-403)[[399]](#cite_note-Dingle_1-404)[[400]](#cite_note-Festival_3-405)

### Literature and media

Main articles: [Scottish literature](/wiki/Scottish_literature "Scottish literature"), [Media of Scotland](/wiki/Media_of_Scotland "Media of Scotland"), [Scottish art](/wiki/Scottish_art "Scottish art"), [Comedy in Scotland](/wiki/Comedy_in_Scotland "Comedy in Scotland"), [Festivals in Scotland](/wiki/Festivals_in_Scotland "Festivals in Scotland"), and [Cinema of Scotland](/wiki/Cinema_of_Scotland "Cinema of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/PG_1063Burns_Naysmithcrop.jpg/250px-PG_1063Burns_Naysmithcrop.jpg)](/wiki/File:PG_1063Burns_Naysmithcrop.jpg)

World renowned poet [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns") is considered the national poet, best known for works such as "[Auld Lang Syne](/wiki/Auld_Lang_Syne "Auld Lang Syne")" and writing in the Scots language

Scotland has a literary heritage dating back to the early Middle Ages. The earliest extant literature composed in what is now Scotland was in [Brythonic](/wiki/Brythonic_languages "Brythonic languages") speech in the 6th century, but is preserved as part of [Welsh literature](/wiki/Welsh-language_literature "Welsh-language literature").[[401]](#cite_note-406) Later medieval literature included works in Latin,[[402]](#cite_note-407) Gaelic,[[403]](#cite_note-408) Old English[[404]](#cite_note-409) and French.[[405]](#cite_note-410) The first surviving major text in [Early Scots](/wiki/Early_Scots "Early Scots") is the 14th-century poet [John Barbour](/wiki/John_Barbour_(poet) "John Barbour (poet)")'s epic *[Brus](/wiki/The_Brus "The Brus")*, focusing on the life of Robert I,[[406]](#cite_note-411) and was soon followed by a series of vernacular romances and prose works.[[407]](#cite_note-Wormald1991pp60-7-412) In the 16th century, the crown's patronage helped the development of Scots drama and poetry,[[408]](#cite_note-Brownetalpp256-7-413) but the accession of James VI to the English throne removed a major centre of literary patronage and Scots was sidelined as a literary language.[[409]](#cite_note-414) Interest in Scots literature was revived in the 18th century by figures including [James Macpherson](/wiki/James_Macpherson "James Macpherson"), whose [Ossian Cycle](/wiki/Ossian "Ossian") made him the first Scottish poet to gain an international reputation and was a major influence on the European Enlightenment.[[410]](#cite_note-415) It was also a major influence on [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), whom many consider the national poet,[[411]](#cite_note-416) and [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"), whose [Waverley Novels](/wiki/Waverley_Novels "Waverley Novels") did much to define Scottish identity in the 19th century.[[412]](#cite_note-417) Towards the end of the Victorian era a number of Scottish-born authors achieved international reputations as writers in English, including [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"), [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle"), [J. M. Barrie](/wiki/J._M._Barrie "J. M. Barrie") and [George MacDonald](/wiki/George_MacDonald "George MacDonald").[[87]](#cite_note-Cultural_Profile-92)

In the 20th century the [Scottish Renaissance](/wiki/Scottish_Renaissance "Scottish Renaissance") saw a surge of literary activity and attempts to reclaim the Scots language as a medium for serious literature.[[413]](#cite_note-VisitingArtsScotland-418) Members of the movement were followed by a new generation of post-war poets including [Edwin Morgan](/wiki/Edwin_Morgan_(poet) "Edwin Morgan (poet)"), who would be appointed the first Makar by the Scottish Government in 2004.[[414]](#cite_note-419) [Sorley MacLean](/wiki/Sorley_MacLean "Sorley MacLean") was described by the [Scottish Poetry Library](/wiki/Scottish_Poetry_Library "Scottish Poetry Library") as "one of the major Scottish poets of the modern era" because of his "mastery of his chosen medium and his engagement with the European poetic tradition and European politics".[[415]](#cite_note-library-420) Nobel Prize Laureate [Seamus Heaney](/wiki/Seamus_Heaney "Seamus Heaney") credited MacLean with saving [Scottish Gaelic poetry](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_literature "Scottish Gaelic literature").[[416]](#cite_note-421) From the 1980s Scottish literature enjoyed another major revival, particularly associated with a group of writers including [Irvine Welsh](/wiki/Irvine_Welsh "Irvine Welsh").[[413]](#cite_note-VisitingArtsScotland-418) Scottish poets who emerged in the same period included [Carol Ann Duffy](/wiki/Carol_Ann_Duffy "Carol Ann Duffy"), who, in May 2009, was the first Scot named the monarch's [Poet Laureate](/wiki/Poet_Laureate "Poet Laureate").[[417]](#cite_note-422) Scotland's national poet is known as the [Makar](/wiki/Makar "Makar") and is appointed by the first minister to promote literacy, writing and poetry across the country, as well as to produce work for national events and produce annual reports to the government.[[418]](#cite_note-423) The incumbent Makar is [Pàdraig MacAoidh](/wiki/P%C3%A0draig_MacAoidh "Pàdraig MacAoidh"), the first Scottish Gaelic writer to hold the position.[[419]](#cite_note-424)

National newspapers such as the [*Daily Record*](/wiki/Daily_Record_(Scotland) "Daily Record (Scotland)"), [*The Herald*](/wiki/The_Herald_(Glasgow) "The Herald (Glasgow)"), *[The Scotsman](/wiki/The_Scotsman "The Scotsman")* and *[The National](/wiki/The_National_(Scotland) "The National (Scotland)")* are all produced in Scotland.[[420]](#cite_note-Newspapers-425) Important regional dailies include the [Evening News](/wiki/Edinburgh_Evening_News "Edinburgh Evening News") in Edinburgh, *[The Courier](/wiki/The_Courier_(Dundee) "The Courier (Dundee)")* in Dundee in the east, and *[The Press and Journal](/wiki/The_Press_and_Journal "The Press and Journal")* serving Aberdeen and the north.[[420]](#cite_note-Newspapers-425) Scotland is represented at the [Celtic Media Festival](/wiki/Celtic_Media_Festival "Celtic Media Festival"), which showcases film and television from the Celtic countries. Scottish entrants have won many awards since the festival began in 1980.[[421]](#cite_note-Media_1-426) Scotland's national broadcaster is [BBC Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Scotland "BBC Scotland"), a division of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), which runs three national [television stations](/wiki/Scottish_television_stations "Scottish television stations"), [BBC One Scotland](/wiki/BBC_One_Scotland "BBC One Scotland"), [BBC Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Scotland_(TV_channel) "BBC Scotland (TV channel)") and the Gaelic-language [BBC Alba](/wiki/BBC_Alba "BBC Alba"), and the national radio stations, *[BBC Radio Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Scotland "BBC Radio Scotland")* and *[BBC Radio nan Gàidheal](/wiki/BBC_Radio_nan_G%C3%A0idheal "BBC Radio nan Gàidheal")*. The main Scottish commercial television station is [STV](/wiki/STV_(TV_network) "STV (TV network)") which broadcasts on two of the three [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(network) "ITV (network)") regions of Scotland.[[422]](#cite_note-427) Scotland's [film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_Scotland "Cinema of Scotland") is supported by [Screen Scotland](/wiki/Creative_Scotland "Creative Scotland"), the [executive non-departmental public body](/wiki/Non-departmental_public_body "Non-departmental public body") of the Scottish Government which provides funding and support for film production in the country.[[423]](#cite_note-428)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Scotland](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland "Music of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Edinburgh_Scottish_bagpiper_%2849552292312%29.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Scottish_bagpiper_%2849552292312%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Scottish_bagpiper_(49552292312).jpg)

The [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes") are an instrument largely associated with Scotland, and an early example of popular Scottish music

[Scottish music](/wiki/Scottish_music "Scottish music") is a significant aspect of the nation's culture, with both traditional and modern influences. A famous traditional Scottish instrument is the [Great Highland bagpipe](/wiki/Great_Highland_bagpipe "Great Highland bagpipe"), a woodwind reed instrument consisting of three drones and a melody pipe (called the chanter), which are fed continuously by a reservoir of air in a bag. The popularity of [pipe bands](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band")—primarily featuring bagpipes, various types of snares and drums, and showcasing Scottish traditional dress and music—has spread throughout the world. Bagpipes are featured in holiday celebrations, parades, funerals, weddings, and other events internationally. Many military regiments have a pipe band of their own. In addition to the Great Highland pipes, several smaller, somewhat quieter [bellows](/wiki/Bellows "Bellows")-blown varieties of bagpipe are played in Scotland, including the [smallpipes](/wiki/Scottish_smallpipes "Scottish smallpipes") and the [Border pipes](/wiki/Border_pipes "Border pipes").

[Scottish popular music](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland#Pop,_rock_and_fusion "Music of Scotland") has gained an international following, with artists such as [Lewis Capaldi](/wiki/Lewis_Capaldi "Lewis Capaldi"), [Amy Macdonald](/wiki/Amy_Macdonald "Amy Macdonald"), [KT Tunstall](/wiki/KT_Tunstall "KT Tunstall"), [Nina Nesbitt](/wiki/Nina_Nesbitt "Nina Nesbitt"), [Chvrches](/wiki/Chvrches "Chvrches"), [Gerry Cinnamon](/wiki/Gerry_Cinnamon "Gerry Cinnamon") and [Paolo Nutini](/wiki/Paolo_Nutini "Paolo Nutini") gaining international success. DJ [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris") was one of the [most streamed artists on Spotify](/wiki/List_of_most-streamed_artists_on_Spotify "List of most-streamed artists on Spotify") in 2023,[[424]](#cite_note-429)[[425]](#cite_note-430) whilst [Susan Boyle](/wiki/Susan_Boyle "Susan Boyle")'s [debut album](/wiki/I_Dreamed_a_Dream_(album) "I Dreamed a Dream (album)") was [one of the best-selling albums of the 21st century](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_of_the_21st_century "List of best-selling albums of the 21st century"), and was the best-selling album internationally in 2009.[[426]](#cite_note-431) [Runrig](/wiki/Runrig "Runrig") popularised [Celtic rock](/wiki/Celtic_rock "Celtic rock") and frequently performed in [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"), with their 1995 single "[An Ubhal as Àirde](/wiki/An_Ubhal_as_%C3%80irde_(The_Highest_Apple) "An Ubhal as Àirde (The Highest Apple)")" the first song in the language to reach the UK Top 20.[[427]](#cite_note-432) Musical talent in Scotland is recognised via the [Scottish Music Awards](/wiki/Scottish_Music_Awards "Scottish Music Awards"), [Scottish Album of the Year Award](/wiki/Scottish_Album_of_the_Year_Award "Scottish Album of the Year Award"), the [Scots Trad Music Awards](/wiki/Scots_Trad_Music_Awards "Scots Trad Music Awards") and the [BBC Radio Scotland Young Traditional Musician](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Scotland_Young_Traditional_Musician "BBC Radio Scotland Young Traditional Musician") award.

### Cuisine

Main article: [Scottish cuisine](/wiki/Scottish_cuisine "Scottish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Haggis_neeps_and_tatties.jpg/250px-Haggis_neeps_and_tatties.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haggis_neeps_and_tatties.jpg)

[Haggis](/wiki/Haggis "Haggis"), [neeps](/wiki/Rutabaga "Rutabaga") and [tatties](/wiki/Potato "Potato")

Scottish cuisine has distinctive attributes and recipes of its own but shares much with wider [British](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine") and [European cuisine](/wiki/European_cuisine "European cuisine") as a result of local and foreign influences, both ancient and modern. Traditional Scottish dishes exist alongside international foodstuffs brought about by migration. Scotland's natural larder of [game](/wiki/Game_(food) "Game (food)"), dairy products, fish, fruit, and vegetables is the chief factor in traditional Scots cooking, with a high reliance on simplicity and a lack of [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice") from abroad, as these were historically rare and expensive.[[428]](#cite_note-433)

[Irn-Bru](/wiki/Irn-Bru "Irn-Bru") is the most common Scottish carbonated soft drink, often described as "Scotland's other national drink" (after whisky).[[429]](#cite_note-434) During the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages") and [early modern era](/wiki/Early_modern_era "Early modern era"), [French cuisine](/wiki/French_cuisine "French cuisine") played a role in Scottish cookery due to cultural exchanges brought about by the "[Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance")",[[430]](#cite_note-435) especially during the reign of [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"). Mary, on her return to Scotland, brought an entourage of French staff who are considered responsible for revolutionising Scots cooking and for some of Scotland's unique food terminology.[[431]](#cite_note-436)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Scotland](/wiki/Sport_in_Scotland "Sport in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Brazil_v_Scotland_25_%285575226993%29.jpg/250px-Brazil_v_Scotland_25_%285575226993%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazil_v_Scotland_25_(5575226993).jpg)

Scotland's [national football team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team")'s fans are commonly known as *The Tartan Army*

Scotland hosts its own national sporting competitions and has independent representation at several international sporting events, including the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), the [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League"), the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"), the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), the [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup"), the [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup"), the [Netball World Cup](/wiki/Netball_World_Cup "Netball World Cup") and the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"). Scotland has its own national governing bodies, such as the [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association") (the second oldest national football association in the world)[[432]](#cite_note-437) and the [Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union"). Variations of football have been played in Scotland for centuries, with the earliest reference dating back to 1424.[[433]](#cite_note-FIFA-438)

The world's first official international association football match, between [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") and [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") was held in Glasgow on 30 November 1872, and resulted in a 0–0 draw.[[434]](#cite_note-439) The [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") was first contested in 1873, and is the oldest trophy in association football.[[435]](#cite_note-440) The [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association") (SFA) is the main [governing body](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") for Scottish association football, and a founding member of the [International Football Association Board](/wiki/International_Football_Association_Board "International Football Association Board") (IFAB) which governs the [Laws of the Game](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)"). Scotland is one of only four countries to have a permanent representative on the IFAB; the other four representatives being appointed for set periods by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA").[[436]](#cite_note-441)[[437]](#cite_note-442) The SFA has responsibility for the [Scotland national football team](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") and the [Scotland women's team](/wiki/Scotland_women%27s_national_football_team "Scotland women's national football team").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg/250px-Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg)

The [Old Course at St Andrews](/wiki/Old_Course_at_St_Andrews "Old Course at St Andrews"), the oldest [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") course in the world

With the modern game of [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf") originating in 15th-century Scotland, the country is promoted as the [home of golf](/wiki/Golf_in_Scotland "Golf in Scotland").[[438]](#cite_note-443)[[439]](#cite_note-444)[[440]](#cite_note-445) To many golfers the [Old Course](/wiki/Old_Course_at_St_Andrews "Old Course at St Andrews") in the Fife town of [St Andrews](/wiki/St_Andrews "St Andrews"), an ancient [links](/wiki/Links_(golf) "Links (golf)") course dating to before 1552,[[441]](#cite_note-446) is considered a site of pilgrimage.[[442]](#cite_note-447) In 1764, the standard 18-hole golf course was created at St Andrews when members modified the course from 22 to 18 holes.[[443]](#cite_note-448) The world's oldest golf tournament, and golf's first major, is [The Open Championship](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship"), which was first played on 17 October 1860 at Prestwick Golf Club, in Ayrshire, Scotland, with [Scottish golfers winning the earliest majors](/wiki/Men%27s_major_golf_championships#Major_championship_winners "Men's major golf championships").[[444]](#cite_note-449) There are many other famous [golf courses in Scotland](/wiki/Golf_in_Scotland "Golf in Scotland"), including [Carnoustie](/wiki/Carnoustie_Golf_Links "Carnoustie Golf Links"), [Gleneagles](/wiki/Gleneagles_(Scotland) "Gleneagles (Scotland)"), [Muirfield](/wiki/Muirfield "Muirfield"), and [Royal Troon](/wiki/Royal_Troon "Royal Troon").

The Scottish Rugby Union is the second oldest rugby union in the world. [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") in Edinburgh is the national stadium of the [Scottish national rugby team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team"). The Scotland rugby team played [their first official](/wiki/1871_Scotland_versus_England_rugby_union_match "1871 Scotland versus England rugby union match") [test match](/wiki/Test_match_(rugby_union) "Test match (rugby union)"), winning 1–0 against England at [Raeburn Place](/wiki/Raeburn_Place "Raeburn Place") in 1871. Scotland has competed in the [Six Nations](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") from the inaugural tournament in 1883, winning it 14 times outright—including the last Five Nations in [1999](/wiki/1999_Five_Nations_Championship "1999 Five Nations Championship")—and sharing it another 8 times. The Rugby World Cup was introduced in 1987 and Scotland have competed in all nine competitions, the most recent being in the [2023 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2023_Rugby_World_Cup "2023 Rugby World Cup"). Scotland competes with the England rugby team annually for the [Calcutta Cup](/wiki/Calcutta_Cup "Calcutta Cup"). Each year, this fixture is played out as part of the Six Nations, with Scotland having last won in 2024.[[445]](#cite_note-450)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg/250px-Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)

[Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium"), the national stadium of the [Scottish national rugby team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team")

Other distinctive features of the national sporting culture include the [Highland games](/wiki/Highland_games "Highland games"), [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling") and [shinty](/wiki/Shinty "Shinty"). In [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), Scotland has had 13 world champions, including [Ken Buchanan](/wiki/Ken_Buchanan "Ken Buchanan"), [Benny Lynch](/wiki/Benny_Lynch "Benny Lynch") and [Jim Watt](/wiki/Jim_Watt_(boxer) "Jim Watt (boxer)"). Scotland has also been successful in [motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport"), particularly in [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"). Notable drivers include; [David Coulthard](/wiki/David_Coulthard "David Coulthard"), [Jim Clark](/wiki/Jim_Clark "Jim Clark"), [Paul Di Resta](/wiki/Paul_Di_Resta "Paul Di Resta"), and [Jackie Stewart](/wiki/Jackie_Stewart "Jackie Stewart").[[446]](#cite_note-451) In [IndyCar](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series"), [Dario Franchitti](/wiki/Dario_Franchitti "Dario Franchitti") has won 4 consecutive [IndyCar world championships](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series").[[447]](#cite_note-452)

Scotland has competed at every [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") since 1930 and has won 356 medals in total—91 Gold, 104 Silver and 161 Bronze.[[448]](#cite_note-453) Scotland has [hosted the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/List_of_Commonwealth_Games_venues "List of Commonwealth Games venues") three times – Edinburgh played host to the Commonwealth Games in [1970](/wiki/1970_British_Commonwealth_Games "1970 British Commonwealth Games") and [1986](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games"), and most recently [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") in [2014](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games").[[449]](#cite_note-454) Glasgow was confirmed as the host city for the [2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2026_Commonwealth_Games "2026 Commonwealth Games") in September 2024.[[450]](#cite_note-455) Edinburgh was the host city for the inaugural [Commonwealth Youth Games in 2000](/wiki/2000_Commonwealth_Youth_Games "2000 Commonwealth Youth Games").[[451]](#cite_note-456)

### National identity

Further information: [Scottish people](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and [National symbols of Scotland](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Scotland "National symbols of Scotland")

[![Royal Arms of Scotland](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Royal_Arms_of_the_Kingdom_of_Scotland.svg/250px-Royal_Arms_of_the_Kingdom_of_Scotland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Arms_of_the_Kingdom_of_Scotland.svg)

The [Royal Arms of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Arms_of_Scotland "Royal Arms of Scotland")

[![Scottish Thistle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Mooie_bloeiwijze_van_een_Speerdistel_%28Cirsium_vulgare%29_03.jpg/250px-Mooie_bloeiwijze_van_een_Speerdistel_%28Cirsium_vulgare%29_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mooie_bloeiwijze_van_een_Speerdistel_(Cirsium_vulgare)_03.jpg)

The [spear thistle](/wiki/Spear_thistle "Spear thistle"), the [national emblem](/wiki/National_emblem "National emblem") of Scotland

The image of [St. Andrew](/wiki/Saint_Andrew "Saint Andrew"), martyred while bound to an X-shaped cross, first appeared in the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") during the reign of [William I](/wiki/William_the_Lion "William the Lion").[[452]](#cite_note-NAS-457) Following the death of [King Alexander III](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") in 1286 an image of Andrew was used on the [seal](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)") of the [Guardians of Scotland](/wiki/Guardians_of_Scotland "Guardians of Scotland") who assumed control of the kingdom during the subsequent [interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum "Interregnum").[[453]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-458) Use of a simplified symbol associated with Saint Andrew, the [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire"), has its origins in the late 14th century; the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") decreeing in 1385 that Scottish soldiers should wear a white Saint Andrew's Cross on the front and back of their tunics.[[454]](#cite_note-459) Use of a blue background for the Saint Andrew's Cross is said to date from at least the 15th century.[[455]](#cite_note-460) Since 1606 the saltire has also formed part of the design of the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag").

There are numerous other symbols and symbolic artefacts, both official and unofficial, including the [thistle](/wiki/Thistle "Thistle"), the nation's [floral emblem](/wiki/National_emblem "National emblem") (celebrated in the song, [The Thistle o' Scotland](/wiki/The_Thistle_o%27_Scotland "The Thistle o' Scotland")), the [Declaration of Arbroath](/wiki/Declaration_of_Arbroath "Declaration of Arbroath"), incorporating a statement of political independence made on 6 April 1320, the textile pattern [tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan") that often signifies a particular [Scottish clan](/wiki/Scottish_clan "Scottish clan") and the royal [Lion Rampant](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_Scotland "Royal Standard of Scotland") flag.[[456]](#cite_note-461)[[457]](#cite_note-462)[[458]](#cite_note-463) Highlanders can thank [James Graham, 3rd Duke of Montrose](/wiki/James_Graham,_3rd_Duke_of_Montrose "James Graham, 3rd Duke of Montrose"), for the repeal in 1782 of the [Dress Act 1746](/wiki/Dress_Act_1746 "Dress Act 1746"), which prohibiting the wearing of tartans.[[459]](#cite_note-Works-464)

Scotland has its own [regalia](/wiki/Regalia "Regalia") known as the [Honours of Scotland](/wiki/Honours_of_Scotland "Honours of Scotland") (informally the [Scottish Crown Jewels](/wiki/Scottish_Crown_Jewels "Scottish Crown Jewels")) which consists of the [Crown of Scotland](/wiki/Crown_of_Scotland "Crown of Scotland"), a Sceptre and [Scottish Sword of State](/wiki/Scottish_Sword_of_State "Scottish Sword of State"). The Scottish crown was worn by [Scottish monarchs](/wiki/Scottish_monarchs "Scottish monarchs") during their coronation, and today is kept in the Crown Room at [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle"). The Crown of Scotland is present at each state opening of the Scottish Parliament.[[460]](#cite_note-465) Collectively, the Honours of Scotland are the oldest regalia in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), dating from the late 15th and early 16th centuries.[[461]](#cite_note-466)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/The_Queen_at_the_Scottish_Parliament_-_crop.jpg/250px-The_Queen_at_the_Scottish_Parliament_-_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Queen_at_the_Scottish_Parliament_-_crop.jpg)

The [Crown of Scotland](/wiki/Crown_of_Scotland "Crown of Scotland")

Although there is no official [national anthem of Scotland](/wiki/National_anthem_of_Scotland "National anthem of Scotland"),[[462]](#cite_note-467) *[Flower of Scotland](/wiki/Flower_of_Scotland "Flower of Scotland")* is played on special occasions and sporting events such as football and rugby matches involving the Scotland national teams and since 2010 is also played at the Commonwealth Games after it was voted the overwhelming favourite by participating Scottish athletes.[[463]](#cite_note-468) Other currently less popular candidates for the National Anthem of Scotland include *[Scotland the Brave](/wiki/Scotland_the_Brave "Scotland the Brave")*, *[Highland Cathedral](/wiki/Highland_Cathedral "Highland Cathedral")*, *[Scots Wha Hae](/wiki/Scots_Wha_Hae "Scots Wha Hae")* and *[A Man's A Man for A' That](/wiki/A_Man%27s_A_Man_for_A%27_That "A Man's A Man for A' That")*.[[464]](#cite_note-469)

[St Andrew's Day](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_Day "St Andrew's Day"), 30 November, is the [national day](/wiki/National_day "National day"), although [Burns' Night](/wiki/Burns%27_Night "Burns' Night") tends to be more widely observed, particularly outside Scotland. In 2006, the Scottish Parliament passed the [St Andrew's Day Bank Holiday (Scotland) Act 2007](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_Day_Bank_Holiday_(Scotland)_Act_2007 "St Andrew's Day Bank Holiday (Scotland) Act 2007"), designating the day an official [bank holiday](/wiki/Bank_holiday "Bank holiday").[[465]](#cite_note-470) [Tartan Day](/wiki/Tartan_Day "Tartan Day") is a recent innovation from Canada.[[466]](#cite_note-471)

The national animal of Scotland is the [unicorn](/wiki/Unicorn "Unicorn"), which has been a Scottish heraldic symbol since the 12th century.[[467]](#cite_note-472) The [Court of the Lord Lyon](/wiki/Court_of_the_Lord_Lyon "Court of the Lord Lyon") regulates [Scottish heraldry](/wiki/Scottish_heraldry "Scottish heraldry") and the Public Register of All Armorial Bearings in Scotland.[[468]](#cite_note-473)

"UK Internal Market Act 2020 imposed new restrictions on the ability of the devolved institutions to enact measures...mutual recognition and non-discrimination requirements mean that standards set by the legislatures in Wales and Scotland cannot restrict the sale of goods which are acceptable in other parts of the UK. London: [Sweet & Maxwell](/wiki/Sweet_%26_Maxwell "Sweet & Maxwell"): 650–676. [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [0023-933X](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/0023-933X). Retrieved 4 November 2023. "Since the act became law on 17 December 2020, the devolved administrations can continue to set standards for goods and services produced within their territory, but their rules do not apply to goods and services coming from other jurisdictions. They also must accept products imported into one part of the UK. This undermines their legislative autonomy and renders certain policies ineffective"

"The UK Internal Market Act gives ministers sweeping powers to enforce mutual recognition and non-discrimination across the four jurisdictions. Existing differences and some social and health matters are exempted but these are much less extensive than the exemptions permitted under the EU Internal Market provisions. Only after an amendment in the House of Lords, the Bill was amended to provide a weak and non-binding consent mechanism for amendments (equivalent to the Sewel Convention) to the list of exemptions. The result is that, while the devolved governments retain regulatory competences, these are undermined by the fact that goods and services originating in, or imported into, England can be marketed anywhere."
209. **[^](#cite_ref-214)** Lydgate, Emily; Anthony, Chloe (September 2022). London: [Wiley](/wiki/Wiley_(publisher) "Wiley (publisher)"): 1168–1190. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1111/1468-2230.12735](https://doi.org/10.1111%2F1468-2230.12735). "While the mutual recognition principle preserves devolved powers, rather than requiring that devolved nations conform with a wide range of harmonised standards (as they did in the EU), the Act undermines devolution simply because devolved legislation will no longer apply to all relevant activity in the devolved territory...Devolution is also undermined by the asymmetry of legislative authority...the UK Internal Market Act is a protected enactment, which devolved administrations are unable to appeal or modify, but which the UK parliament will be able to modify when legislating for England."

"The market access principles undermine devolved competences in two ways...[they] significantly undermine the purpose of devolution, which was to enable the devolved nations and regions to legislate according to their own local needs and political preferences."

"So when used to disapply relevant requirements in a destination devolved jurisdiction the effect is different from that generated by the devolution statutes when they treat rules that are outside of competence as being 'not law'. In this way, the legislative competence of each jurisdiction is formally maintained, but its exercise constrained by the extraterritorial reach of regulatory norms applicable elsewhere in the UK and by the potential for regulatory competition where local producers are subject to local rules but competing goods can enter that market in compliance with the regulatory standards from where they originate...the UKIM Act 2020 allows extraterritorial application of rules that reflect different preferences or even undermines local preferences through regulatory competition, its effects are not insignificant for devolved legislatures."
